[[ja:%E7%94%B0%E5%90%8D%E9%83%A8%E3%81%BE%E3%81%A4%E3%82%8A]] [[File:Aomori- Tanabu Festival-m.jpg|thumb|270px|Tanabu Festival]] The '''Tanabu Festival''' (田名部まつり, *Tanabu Matsuri*) is the '''annual grand festival''' (*Reitaisai*) of '''Tanabu Shrine''' in '''{{ill|Mutsu, Aomori|ja|むつ市}}'''. It has been designated as an '''intangible folk cultural property''' (*Mukei Minzoku Bunkazai*) of '''{{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県}}'''.
== History and Background == * The '''origin''' of the '''Tanabu Festival''' is '''uncertain''', but it is said to have a '''history of over 370 years'''{{fact|date=August 2023}}.
* According to '''"Maki no Asatsuyu"''', written by the '''Edo-period traveler {{ill|Sugao Masumi|ja|菅江真澄}}''' during his visit to Tanabu, the festival was already being held in a '''form similar to the present-day event by 1793''' (*Kansei 5*).
* '''Tanabu''' once housed the '''daikansho (magistrate's office)''' of the '''{{ill|Nanbu Domain|ja|南部藩}}''', making it the '''center of industry, economy, and culture''' in '''Shimokita'''. Although Tanabu itself did not have a '''seaport''', goods from '''{{ill|Kitamaebune|ja|北前船}}''' ships docked in '''{{ill|Mutsu Bay|ja|陸奥湾}}''' (*Ōminato Bay*), were transported '''via the Tanabu River''', and helped the town flourish.
* Shimokita exported '''Hiba cypress, abalone, sea cucumber, and kelp''' to the Kansai region, while rare goods arrived from various parts of Japan via the '''West-bound shipping route''' (*Saikairi Kairō*). Along with these goods, '''language and culture''' were also introduced to the region.
* Shimokita’s '''products were highly valued''' as '''luxury goods''' and sold as '''Nagasaki Tawaramono''' (*high-end exports to China*). This trade brought '''wealth''' to the townspeople. Though Shimokita was sometimes called a '''"barren land"''', the people could '''simply buy''' food when they couldn't grow their own. It is even said that the '''people of Shimokita did not struggle to eat''' and had the leisure to '''enjoy poetry, calligraphy, and education'''.
* Since the '''early Edo period''', many '''{{ill|Ōmi merchants|ja|近江商人}}''' were active in Shimokita, playing a major role in '''spreading language and culture'''. It is believed that through these merchants—who came from present-day '''Shiga Prefecture'''—the '''traditions of Kyoto's {{ill|Gion Festival|ja|祇園祭}}''' were introduced to Tanabu. This influence is evident in the '''festival floats (Yama)''' and '''festival music''', earning Tanabu Festival the nickname '''"Miyabi of the North"''' (*北のみやび*).
* The '''night festival music (Yamaya-re)''' resembles the '''Chichibu Night Festival''' and other '''Kantō-style''' festival melodies, suggesting influences from regions beyond Kyoto and Shiga.
* The '''daytime decorations''' reflect the '''extravagance of Kyoto’s Gion Festival''', with some items made '''in Kyoto''' or even '''imported from China and Persia'''. Meanwhile, the '''nighttime decorations''', such as the '''Mikaeri-e (rear-facing paintings)''', are influenced by '''Nebuta festivals''' from '''Tsugaru and Shimokita''' and are crafted locally.
* The origins of the '''tradition of Tanabu Festival''' remain unclear, but one thing is certain: without the '''economic power of the townspeople''', they could not have afforded '''lavish festival floats, ornate furnishings, and sacred objects'''. Likewise, without '''community spirit and pride''', they could not have '''maintained and passed down''' these traditions.
* The festival was '''originally held on the lunar calendar dates of July 18-20''', but in '''1870 (Meiji 3),''' it was '''changed to the current dates of August 18-20'''.
== Festival Floats (Yama) == The '''five districts of Tanabu''' (*Tanabu 5-chō*: '''Yokomukaichō, Ogawachō, Yanagimachi, Honmachi, and Shinmachi''') each have '''a traditional wooden festival float''', known as '''"Yama"''' (山車). These floats are '''black-lacquered, two-story structures'''.
- The '''lower level''' houses the '''musicians''' (*Noriko*, 囃子方). - The '''upper level''' carries '''the enshrined deity''' (*Goshintai*, 御神体). - Each '''Yama''' has '''unique carvings, lacquer paintings, roof styles, and decorative metal fittings'''. - The '''wheels are wooden''', and '''turning corners''' is difficult due to the '''height''' of the Yama, requiring '''skilled handlers (Teko, 梃子)''' to rotate the floats at intersections. * '''Thick ropes''' are attached to the '''Yama''', and they are '''pulled by hand'''.
The '''decorations change between day and night''': * '''Daytime:''' The '''Yama''' is adorned with '''embroidered curtains''' (*Ōmizuhiki-maku, Komizuhiki-maku, Mikaeri-maku, and Misu*). These textiles were traditionally '''ordered from Kyoto''' or '''imported from China via Nagasaki'''. * '''Nighttime:''' The curtains are replaced with '''illuminated painted panels (Gaku, 額)''', drawn by '''local artists''' and '''redesigned every year'''.
The festival '''groups (Kumi, 組)''' manage the '''Yama operation''', following a '''strict hierarchy''': * '''Kumi Leader (組頭, Kumitō)''' * '''Vice Leader (副組頭)''' * '''Sub-leaders (小頭, Kojirō)''' * '''Councilors (理事, Rijī)''' * '''Executive members (幹事, Kanjī)''' - Members '''"retire" at around age 40'''.
== List of Floats == === Lead Float: Inari Yama (稲荷山) === * '''Yokomukaichō Gōsen-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Inari|ja|稲荷神}} & a '''pair of white foxes''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Immortal riding a yellow crane'''
=== Second Float: Shōjō Yama (猩猩山) === * '''Ogawachō Giyū-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Shōjō|ja|猩猩}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Phoenix carrying an immortal'''
=== Third Float: Daikoku Yama (大黒山) === * '''Yanagimachi Kyōshin-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Daikokuten|ja|大黒天}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Yoshimitsu receiving a secret tune'''
=== Fourth Float: Ebisu Yama (蛭子山) === * '''Honmachi & Tanabu-chō Myōsei-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Ebisu|ja|蛭子}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Great turtle carrying an immortal'''
=== Final Float: Kōro Hō (香爐峯) === * '''Shinmachi Shinsei-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Sei Shōnagon|ja|清少納言}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Oniwakamaru riding a koi fish'''
== Festival Music and Chants == The music features '''flutes, drums, gongs, and taiko'''. Two major musical styles: * '''Gion-bayashi''' (*Gion Music*): Played '''during daytime''', characterized by '''a melancholic, elegant melody'''. * '''Yamaya-re''' (*Nighttime music*): A '''fast-paced, elaborate flute melody''' played at night.
Different '''chants''' are used for float turns: * '''Gōsen-gumi (Inari Yama):''' "Todoro-tōnō" * '''Giyū-gumi (Shōjō Yama):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō" * '''Kyōshin-gumi (Daikoku Yama):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō" * '''Myōsei-gumi (Ebisu Yama):''' "Todoro-se-nō" * '''Shinsei-gumi (Kōro Hō):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō"
== Schedule == Held annually '''August 18-20'''.
== External Links == - [http://www.city.mutsu.lg.jp/ Tanabu Shrine Grand Festival – Mutsu City Official Website]

[[ja:%E7%94%B0%E5%90%8D%E9%83%A8%E3%81%BE%E3%81%A4%E3%82%8A]] [[File:Aomori- Tanabu Festival-m.jpg|thumb|270px|Tanabu Festival]] The '''Tanabu Festival''' (田名部まつり, *Tanabu Matsuri*) is the '''annual grand festival''' (*Reitaisai*) of '''Tanabu Shrine''' in '''{{ill|Mutsu, Aomori|ja|むつ市}}'''. It has been designated as an '''intangible folk cultural property''' (*Mukei Minzoku Bunkazai*) of '''{{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県}}'''.
== History and Background == * The '''origin''' of the '''Tanabu Festival''' is '''uncertain''', but it is said to have a '''history of over 370 years'''{{fact|date=August 2023}}.
* According to '''"Maki no Asatsuyu"''', written by the '''Edo-period traveler {{ill|Sugao Masumi|ja|菅江真澄}}''' during his visit to Tanabu, the festival was already being held in a '''form similar to the present-day event by 1793''' (*Kansei 5*).
* '''Tanabu''' once housed the '''daikansho (magistrate's office)''' of the '''{{ill|Nanbu Domain|ja|南部藩}}''', making it the '''center of industry, economy, and culture''' in '''Shimokita'''. Although Tanabu itself did not have a '''seaport''', goods from '''{{ill|Kitamaebune|ja|北前船}}''' ships docked in '''{{ill|Mutsu Bay|ja|陸奥湾}}''' (*Ōminato Bay*), were transported '''via the Tanabu River''', and helped the town flourish.
* Shimokita exported '''Hiba cypress, abalone, sea cucumber, and kelp''' to the Kansai region, while rare goods arrived from various parts of Japan via the '''West-bound shipping route''' (*Saikairi Kairō*). Along with these goods, '''language and culture''' were also introduced to the region.
* Shimokita’s '''products were highly valued''' as '''luxury goods''' and sold as '''Nagasaki Tawaramono''' (*high-end exports to China*). This trade brought '''wealth''' to the townspeople. Though Shimokita was sometimes called a '''"barren land"''', the people could '''simply buy''' food when they couldn't grow their own. It is even said that the '''people of Shimokita did not struggle to eat''' and had the leisure to '''enjoy poetry, calligraphy, and education'''.
* Since the '''early Edo period''', many '''{{ill|Ōmi merchants|ja|近江商人}}''' were active in Shimokita, playing a major role in '''spreading language and culture'''. It is believed that through these merchants—who came from present-day '''Shiga Prefecture'''—the '''traditions of Kyoto's {{ill|Gion Festival|ja|祇園祭}}''' were introduced to Tanabu. This influence is evident in the '''festival floats (Yama)''' and '''festival music''', earning Tanabu Festival the nickname '''"Miyabi of the North"''' (*北のみやび*).
* The '''night festival music (Yamaya-re)''' resembles the '''Chichibu Night Festival''' and other '''Kantō-style''' festival melodies, suggesting influences from regions beyond Kyoto and Shiga.
* The '''daytime decorations''' reflect the '''extravagance of Kyoto’s Gion Festival''', with some items made '''in Kyoto''' or even '''imported from China and Persia'''. Meanwhile, the '''nighttime decorations''', such as the '''Mikaeri-e (rear-facing paintings)''', are influenced by '''Nebuta festivals''' from '''Tsugaru and Shimokita''' and are crafted locally.
* The origins of the '''tradition of Tanabu Festival''' remain unclear, but one thing is certain: without the '''economic power of the townspeople''', they could not have afforded '''lavish festival floats, ornate furnishings, and sacred objects'''. Likewise, without '''community spirit and pride''', they could not have '''maintained and passed down''' these traditions.
* The festival was '''originally held on the lunar calendar dates of July 18-20''', but in '''1870 (Meiji 3),''' it was '''changed to the current dates of August 18-20'''.
== Festival Floats (Yama) == The '''five districts of Tanabu''' (*Tanabu 5-chō*: '''Yokomukaichō, Ogawachō, Yanagimachi, Honmachi, and Shinmachi''') each have '''a traditional wooden festival float''', known as '''"Yama"''' (山車). These floats are '''black-lacquered, two-story structures'''.
- The '''lower level''' houses the '''musicians''' (*Noriko*, 囃子方). - The '''upper level''' carries '''the enshrined deity''' (*Goshintai*, 御神体). - Each '''Yama''' has '''unique carvings, lacquer paintings, roof styles, and decorative metal fittings'''. - The '''wheels are wooden''', and '''turning corners''' is difficult due to the '''height''' of the Yama, requiring '''skilled handlers (Teko, 梃子)''' to rotate the floats at intersections. * '''Thick ropes''' are attached to the '''Yama''', and they are '''pulled by hand'''.
The '''decorations change between day and night''': * '''Daytime:''' The '''Yama''' is adorned with '''embroidered curtains''' (*Ōmizuhiki-maku, Komizuhiki-maku, Mikaeri-maku, and Misu*). These textiles were traditionally '''ordered from Kyoto''' or '''imported from China via Nagasaki'''. * '''Nighttime:''' The curtains are replaced with '''illuminated painted panels (Gaku, 額)''', drawn by '''local artists''' and '''redesigned every year'''.
The festival '''groups (Kumi, 組)''' manage the '''Yama operation''', following a '''strict hierarchy''': * '''Kumi Leader (組頭, Kumitō)''' * '''Vice Leader (副組頭)''' * '''Sub-leaders (小頭, Kojirō)''' * '''Councilors (理事, Rijī)''' * '''Executive members (幹事, Kanjī)''' - Members '''"retire" at around age 40'''.
== List of Floats == === Lead Float: Inari Yama (稲荷山) === * '''Yokomukaichō Gōsen-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Inari|ja|稲荷神}} & a '''pair of white foxes''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Immortal riding a yellow crane'''
=== Second Float: Shōjō Yama (猩猩山) === * '''Ogawachō Giyū-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Shōjō|ja|猩猩}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Phoenix carrying an immortal'''
=== Third Float: Daikoku Yama (大黒山) === * '''Yanagimachi Kyōshin-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Daikokuten|ja|大黒天}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Yoshimitsu receiving a secret tune'''
=== Fourth Float: Ebisu Yama (蛭子山) === * '''Honmachi & Tanabu-chō Myōsei-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Ebisu|ja|蛭子}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Great turtle carrying an immortal'''
=== Final Float: Kōro Hō (香爐峯) === * '''Shinmachi Shinsei-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Sei Shōnagon|ja|清少納言}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Oniwakamaru riding a koi fish'''
== Festival Music and Chants == The music features '''flutes, drums, gongs, and taiko'''. Two major musical styles: * '''Gion-bayashi''' (*Gion Music*): Played '''during daytime''', characterized by '''a melancholic, elegant melody'''. * '''Yamaya-re''' (*Nighttime music*): A '''fast-paced, elaborate flute melody''' played at night.
Different '''chants''' are used for float turns: * '''Gōsen-gumi (Inari Yama):''' "Todoro-tōnō" * '''Giyū-gumi (Shōjō Yama):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō" * '''Kyōshin-gumi (Daikoku Yama):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō" * '''Myōsei-gumi (Ebisu Yama):''' "Todoro-se-nō" * '''Shinsei-gumi (Kōro Hō):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō"
== Schedule == Held annually '''August 18-20'''.
== External Links == - [http://www.city.mutsu.lg.jp/ Tanabu Shrine Grand Festival – Mutsu City Official Website]

* '''Tanabu''' once housed the '''daikansho (magistrate's office)''' of the '''{{ill|Nanbu Domain|ja|南部藩}}''', making it the '''center of industry, economy, and culture''' in '''Shimokita'''. Although Tanabu itself did not have a '''seaport''', goods from '''{{ill|Kitamaebune|ja|北前船}}''' ships docked in '''{{ill|Mutsu Bay|ja|陸奥湾}}''' (*Ōminato Bay*), were transported '''via the Tanabu River''', and helped the town flourish.
* Shimokita exported '''Hiba cypress, abalone, sea cucumber, and kelp''' to the Kansai region, while rare goods arrived from various parts of Japan via the '''West-bound shipping route''' (*Saikairi Kairō*). Along with these goods, '''language and culture''' were also introduced to the region.
* Shimokita’s '''products were highly valued''' as '''luxury goods''' and sold as '''Nagasaki Tawaramono''' (*high-end exports to China*). This trade brought '''wealth''' to the townspeople. Though Shimokita was sometimes called a '''"barren land"''', the people could '''simply buy''' food when they couldn't grow their own. It is even said that the '''people of Shimokita did not struggle to eat''' and had the leisure to '''enjoy poetry, calligraphy, and education'''.
* Since the '''early Edo period''', many '''{{ill|Ōmi merchants|ja|近江Merchant}}''' were active in Shimokita, playing a major role in '''spreading language and culture'''. It is believed that through these merchants—who came from present-day '''Shiga Prefecture'''—the '''traditions of Kyoto's {{ill|Gion Festival|ja|祇園祭}}''' were introduced to Tanabu. This influence is evident in the '''festival floats (Yama)''' and '''festival music''', earning Tanabu Festival the nickname '''"Miyabi of the North"''' (*北のみやび*).
* The '''night festival music (Yamaya-re)''' resembles the '''Chichibu Night Festival''' and other '''Kantō-style''' festival melodies, suggesting influences from regions beyond Kyoto and Shiga.
* The '''daytime decorations''' reflect the '''extravagance of Kyoto’s Gion Festival''', with some items made '''in Kyoto''' or even '''imported from China and Persia'''. Meanwhile, the '''nighttime decorations''', such as the '''Mikaeri-e (rear-facing paintings)''', are influenced by '''Nebuta festivals''' from '''Tsugaru and Shimokita''' and are crafted locally.
* The origins of the '''tradition of Tanabu Festival''' remain unclear, but one thing is certain: without the '''economic power of the townspeople''', they could not have afforded '''lavish festival floats, ornate furnishings, and sacred objects'''. Likewise, without '''community spirit and pride''', they could not have '''maintained and passed down''' these traditions.
* The festival was '''originally held on the lunar calendar dates of July 18-20''', but in '''1870 (Meiji 3),''' it was '''changed to the current dates of August 18-20'''.
== Festival Floats (Yama) == The '''five districts of Tanabu''' (*Tanabu 5-chō*: '''Yokomukaichō, Ogawachō, Yanagimachi, Honmachi, and Shinmachi''') each have '''a traditional wooden festival float''', known as '''"Yama"''' (山車). These floats are '''black-lacquered, two-story structures'''.
- The '''lower level''' houses the '''musicians''' (*Noriko*, 囃子方). - The '''upper level''' carries '''the enshrined deity''' (*Goshintai*, 御神体). - Each '''Yama''' has '''unique carvings, lacquer paintings, roof styles, and decorative metal fittings'''. - The '''wheels are wooden''', and '''turning corners''' is difficult due to the '''height''' of the Yama, requiring '''skilled handlers (Teko, 梃子)''' to rotate the floats at intersections. * '''Thick ropes''' are attached to the '''Yama''', and they are '''pulled by hand'''.
The '''decorations change between day and night''': * '''Daytime:''' The '''Yama''' is adorned with '''embroidered curtains''' (*Ōmizuhiki-maku, Komizuhiki-maku, Mikaeri-maku, and Misu*). These textiles were traditionally '''ordered from Kyoto''' or '''imported from China via Nagasaki'''. * '''Nighttime:''' The curtains are replaced with '''illuminated painted panels (Gaku, 額)''', drawn by '''local artists''' and '''redesigned every year'''.
The festival '''groups (Kumi, 組)''' manage the '''Yama operation''', following a '''strict hierarchy''': * '''Kumi Leader (組頭, Kumitō)''' * '''Vice Leader (副組頭)''' * '''Sub-leaders (小頭, Kojirō)''' * '''Councilors (理事, Rijī)''' * '''Executive members (幹事, Kanjī)''' - Members '''"retire" at around age 40'''.
== List of Floats == === Lead Float: Inari Yama (稲荷山) === * '''Yokomukaichō Gōsen-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Inari|ja|稲荷神}} & a '''pair of white foxes''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Immortal riding a yellow crane'''
=== Second Float: Shōjō Yama (猩猩山) === * '''Ogawachō Giyū-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Shōjō|ja|猩猩}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Phoenix carrying an immortal'''
=== Third Float: Daikoku Yama (大黒山) === * '''Yanagimachi Kyōshin-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Daikokuten|ja|大黒天}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Yoshimitsu receiving a secret tune'''
=== Fourth Float: Ebisu Yama (蛭子山) === * '''Honmachi & Tanabu-chō Myōsei-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Ebisu|ja|蛭子}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Great turtle carrying an immortal'''
=== Final Float: Kōro Hō (香爐峯) === * '''Shinmachi Shinsei-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Sei Shōnagon|ja|清少納言}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Oniwakamaru riding a koi fish'''
== Festival Music and Chants == The music features '''flutes, drums, gongs, and taiko'''. Two major musical styles: * '''Gion-bayashi''' (*Gion Music*): Played '''during daytime''', characterized by '''a melancholic, elegant melody'''. * '''Yamaya-re''' (*Nighttime music*): A '''fast-paced, elaborate flute melody''' played at night.
Different '''chants''' are used for float turns: * '''Gōsen-gumi (Inari Yama):''' "Todoro-tōnō" * '''Giyū-gumi (Shōjō Yama):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō" * '''Kyōshin-gumi (Daikoku Yama):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō" * '''Myōsei-gumi (Ebisu Yama):''' "Todoro-se-nō" * '''Shinsei-gumi (Kōro Hō):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō"
== Schedule == Held annually '''August 18-20'''.
== External Links == - [http://www.city.mutsu.lg.jp/ Tanabu Shrine Grand Festival – Mutsu City Official Website]
{{DEFAULTSORT:Tanabu Festival}} [[Category:Aomori Festivals]] [[Category:Traditional Japanese Festivals]] [[Category:Japanese Float Festivals]] [[Category:Mutsu, Aomori]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tanabu Festival]][[Category:qq]] [[Category: tier 6]][[Category: tier 6]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tanabu Festival]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tanabu Festival]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] <!--Tanabu Festival-->[[Category:Tanabu Festival]][[Category:Tanabu Festival]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tanabu Festival]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Tanabu Festival]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Tanabu Festival]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tanabu Festival]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]]

* According to '''"Maki no Asatsuyu"''', written by the '''Edo-period traveler {{ill|Sugao Masumi|ja|菅江真澄}}''' during his visit to Tanabu, the festival was already being held in a '''form similar to the present-day event by 1793''' (*Kansei 5*).

* Shimokita’s '''products were highly valued''' as '''luxury goods''' and sold as '''Nagasaki Tawaramono''' (*high-end exports to China*). This trade brought '''wealth''' to the townspeople. Though Shimokita was sometimes called a '''"barren land"''', the people could '''simply buy''' food when they couldn't grow their own. It is even said that the '''people of Shimokita did not struggle to eat''' and had the leisure to '''enjoy poetry, calligraphy, and education'''.

The '''decorations change between day and night''': * '''Daytime:''' The '''Yama''' is adorned with '''embroidered curtains''' (*Ōmizuhiki-maku, Komizuhiki-maku, Mikaeri-maku, and Misu*). These textiles were traditionally '''ordered from Kyoto''' or '''imported from China via Nagasaki'''. * '''Nighttime:''' The curtains are replaced with '''illuminated painted panels (Gaku, 額)''', drawn by '''local artists''' and '''redesigned every year'''.
The festival '''groups (Kumi, 組)''' manage the '''Yama operation''', following a '''strict hierarchy''': * '''Kumi Leader (組頭, Kumitō)''' * '''Vice Leader (副組頭)''' * '''Sub-leaders (小頭, Kojirō)''' * '''Councilors (理事, Rijī)''' * '''Executive members (幹事, Kanjī)''' - Members '''"retire" at around age 40'''.

[[File:Aomori- Tanabu Festival-m.jpg|thumb|270px|Tanabu Festival]] The '''Tanabu Festival''' (田名部まつり, *Tanabu Matsuri*) is the '''annual grand festival''' (*Reitaisai*) of '''Tanabu Shrine''' in '''{{ill|Mutsu, Aomori|ja|むつ市}}'''. Though Shimokita was sometimes called a '''"barren land"''', the people could '''simply buy''' food when they couldn't grow their own. It is even said that the '''people of Shimokita did not struggle to eat''' and had the leisure to '''enjoy poetry, calligraphy, and education'''.
* Since the '''early Edo period''', many '''{{ill|Ōmi merchants|ja|近江Merchant}}''' were active in Shimokita, playing a major role in '''spreading language and culture'''. It is believed that through these merchants—who came from present-day '''Shiga Prefecture'''—the '''traditions of Kyoto's {{ill|Gion Festival|ja|祇園祭}}''' were introduced to Tanabu. This influence is evident in the '''festival floats (Yama)''' and '''festival music''', earning Tanabu Festival the nickname '''"Miyabi of the North"''' (*北のみやび*).
* The '''night festival music (Yamaya-re)''' resembles the '''Chichibu Night Festival''' and other '''Kantō-style''' festival melodies, suggesting influences from regions beyond Kyoto and Shiga.
* The '''daytime decorations''' reflect the '''extravagance of Kyoto’s Gion Festival''', with some items made '''in Kyoto''' or even '''imported from China and Persia'''. Meanwhile, the '''nighttime decorations''', such as the '''Mikaeri-e (rear-facing paintings)''', are influenced by '''Nebuta festivals''' from '''Tsugaru and Shimokita''' and are crafted locally.
* The origins of the '''tradition of Tanabu Festival''' remain unclear, but one thing is certain: without the '''economic power of the townspeople''', they could not have afforded '''lavish festival floats, ornate furnishings, and sacred objects'''. Likewise, without '''community spirit and pride''', they could not have '''maintained and passed down''' these traditions.
* The festival was '''originally held on the lunar calendar dates of July 18-20''', but in '''1870 (Meiji 3),''' it was '''changed to the current dates of August 18-20'''.
== Festival Floats (Yama) == The '''five districts of Tanabu''' (*Tanabu 5-chō*: '''Yokomukaichō, Ogawachō, Yanagimachi, Honmachi, and Shinmachi''') each have '''a traditional wooden festival float''', known as '''"Yama"''' (山車). These floats are '''black-lacquered, two-story structures'''.
- The '''lower level''' houses the '''musicians''' (*Noriko*, 囃子方). - The '''upper level''' carries '''the enshrined deity''' (*Goshintai*, 御神体). - Each '''Yama''' has '''unique carvings, lacquer paintings, roof styles, and decorative metal fittings'''. - The '''wheels are wooden''', and '''turning corners''' is difficult due to the '''height''' of the Yama, requiring '''skilled handlers (Teko, 梃子)''' to rotate the floats at intersections. * '''Thick ropes''' are attached to the '''Yama''', and they are '''pulled by hand'''.
The '''decorations change between day and night''': * '''Daytime:''' The '''Yama''' is adorned with '''embroidered curtains''' (*Ōmizuhiki-maku, Komizuhiki-maku, Mikaeri-maku, and Misu*). These textiles were traditionally '''ordered from Kyoto''' or '''imported from China via Nagasaki'''. * '''Nighttime:''' The curtains are replaced with '''illuminated painted panels (Gaku, 額)''', drawn by '''local artists''' and '''redesigned every year'''.
The festival '''groups (Kumi, 組)''' manage the '''Yama operation''', following a '''strict hierarchy''': * '''Kumi Leader (組頭, Kumitō)''' * '''Vice Leader (副組頭)''' * '''Sub-leaders (小頭, Kojirō)''' * '''Councilors (理事, Rijī)''' * '''Executive members (幹事, Kanjī)''' - Members '''"retire" at around age 40'''.
== List of Floats == === Lead Float: Inari Yama (稲荷山) === * '''Yokomukaichō Gōsen-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Inari|ja|稲荷神}} & a '''pair of white foxes''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Immortal riding a yellow crane'''
=== Second Float: Shōjō Yama (猩猩山) === * '''Ogawachō Giyū-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Shōjō|ja|猩猩}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Phoenix carrying an immortal'''
=== Third Float: Daikoku Yama (大黒山) === * '''Yanagimachi Kyōshin-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Daikokuten|ja|大黒天}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Yoshimitsu receiving a secret tune'''
=== Fourth Float: Ebisu Yama (蛭子山) === * '''Honmachi & Tanabu-chō Myōsei-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Ebisu|ja|蛭子}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Great turtle carrying an immortal'''
=== Final Float: Kōro Hō (香爐峯) === * '''Shinmachi Shinsei-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Sei Shōnagon|ja|清少納言}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Oniwakamaru riding a koi fish'''
== Festival Music and Chants == The music features '''flutes, drums, gongs, and taiko'''. Two major musical styles: * '''Gion-bayashi''' (*Gion Music*): Played '''during daytime''', characterized by '''a melancholic, elegant melody'''. * '''Yamaya-re''' (*Nighttime music*): A '''fast-paced, elaborate flute melody''' played at night.
Different '''chants''' are used for float turns: * '''Gōsen-gumi (Inari Yama):''' "Todoro-tōnō" * '''Giyū-gumi (Shōjō Yama):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō" * '''Kyōshin-gumi (Daikoku Yama):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō" * '''Myōsei-gumi (Ebisu Yama):''' "Todoro-se-nō" * '''Shinsei-gumi (Kōro Hō):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō"
== Schedule == Held annually '''August 18-20'''.
== External Links == - [http://www.city.mutsu.lg.jp/ Tanabu Shrine Grand Festival – Mutsu City Official Website]

[[File:Aomori- Tanabu Festival-m.jpg|thumb|270px|Tanabu Festival]] The '''Tanabu Festival''' (田名部まつり, *Tanabu Matsuri*) is the '''annual grand festival''' (*Reitaisai*) of '''Tanabu Shrine''' in '''{{ill|Mutsu, Aomori|ja|むつ市|12=draft}}'''. It has been designated as an '''intangible folk cultural property''' (*Mukei Minzoku Bunkazai*) of '''{{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県|12=draft}}'''.
== History and Background == * The '''origin''' of the '''Tanabu Festival''' is '''uncertain''', but it is said to have a '''history of over 370 years'''{{fact|date=August 2023}}.
* According to '''"Maki no Asatsuyu"''', written by the '''Edo-period traveler {{ill|Sugao Masumi|ja|菅江真澄|12=draft}}''' during his visit to Tanabu, the festival was already being held in a '''form similar to the present-day event by 1793''' (*Kansei 5*).
* '''Tanabu''' once housed the '''daikansho (magistrate's office)''' of the '''{{ill|Nanbu Domain|ja|南部藩|12=draft}}''', making it the '''center of industry, economy, and culture''' in '''Shimokita'''. Although Tanabu itself did not have a '''seaport''', goods from '''{{ill|Kitamaebune|ja|北前船|12=draft}}''' ships docked in '''{{ill|Mutsu Bay|ja|陸奥湾|12=draft}}''' (*Ōminato Bay*), were transported '''via the Tanabu River''', and helped the town flourish.
* Shimokita exported '''Hiba cypress, abalone, sea cucumber, and kelp''' to the Kansai region, while rare goods arrived from various parts of Japan via the '''West-bound shipping route''' (*Saikairi Kairō*). Along with these goods, '''language and culture''' were also introduced to the region.
* Shimokita’s '''products were highly valued''' as '''luxury goods''' and sold as '''Nagasaki Tawaramono''' (*high-end exports to China*). This trade brought '''wealth''' to the townspeople. Though Shimokita was sometimes called a '''"barren land"''', the people could '''simply buy''' food when they couldn't grow their own. It is even said that the '''people of Shimokita did not struggle to eat''' and had the leisure to '''enjoy poetry, calligraphy, and education'''.
* Since the '''early Edo period''', many '''{{ill|Ōmi merchants|ja|近江Merchant|12=draft}}''' were active in Shimokita, playing a major role in '''spreading language and culture'''. It is believed that through these merchants—who came from present-day '''Shiga Prefecture'''—the '''traditions of Kyoto's {{ill|Gion Festival|ja|祇園祭|12=draft}}''' were introduced to Tanabu. This influence is evident in the '''festival floats (Yama)''' and '''festival music''', earning Tanabu Festival the nickname '''"Miyabi of the North"''' (*北のみやび*).
* The '''night festival music (Yamaya-re)''' resembles the '''Chichibu Night Festival''' and other '''Kantō-style''' festival melodies, suggesting influences from regions beyond Kyoto and Shiga.
* The '''daytime decorations''' reflect the '''extravagance of Kyoto’s Gion Festival''', with some items made '''in Kyoto''' or even '''imported from China and Persia'''. Meanwhile, the '''nighttime decorations''', such as the '''Mikaeri-e (rear-facing paintings)''', are influenced by '''Nebuta festivals''' from '''Tsugaru and Shimokita''' and are crafted locally.
* The origins of the '''tradition of Tanabu Festival''' remain unclear, but one thing is certain: without the '''economic power of the townspeople''', they could not have afforded '''lavish festival floats, ornate furnishings, and sacred objects'''. Likewise, without '''community spirit and pride''', they could not have '''maintained and passed down''' these traditions.
* The festival was '''originally held on the lunar calendar dates of July 18-20''', but in '''1870 (Meiji 3),''' it was '''changed to the current dates of August 18-20'''.
== Festival Floats (Yama) == The '''five districts of Tanabu''' (*Tanabu 5-chō*: '''Yokomukaichō, Ogawachō, Yanagimachi, Honmachi, and Shinmachi''') each have '''a traditional wooden festival float''', known as '''"Yama"''' (山車). These floats are '''black-lacquered, two-story structures'''.
- The '''lower level''' houses the '''musicians''' (*Noriko*, 囃子方). - The '''upper level''' carries '''the enshrined deity''' (*Goshintai*, 御神体). - Each '''Yama''' has '''unique carvings, lacquer paintings, roof styles, and decorative metal fittings'''. - The '''wheels are wooden''', and '''turning corners''' is difficult due to the '''height''' of the Yama, requiring '''skilled handlers (Teko, 梃子)''' to rotate the floats at intersections. * '''Thick ropes''' are attached to the '''Yama''', and they are '''pulled by hand'''.
The '''decorations change between day and night''': * '''Daytime:''' The '''Yama''' is adorned with '''embroidered curtains''' (*Ōmizuhiki-maku, Komizuhiki-maku, Mikaeri-maku, and Misu*). These textiles were traditionally '''ordered from Kyoto''' or '''imported from China via Nagasaki'''. * '''Nighttime:''' The curtains are replaced with '''illuminated painted panels (Gaku, 額)''', drawn by '''local artists''' and '''redesigned every year'''.
The festival '''groups (Kumi, 組)''' manage the '''Yama operation''', following a '''strict hierarchy''': * '''Kumi Leader (組頭, Kumitō)''' * '''Vice Leader (副組頭)''' * '''Sub-leaders (小頭, Kojirō)''' * '''Councilors (理事, Rijī)''' * '''Executive members (幹事, Kanjī)''' - Members '''"retire" at around age 40'''.
== List of Floats == === Lead Float: Inari Yama (稲荷山) === * '''Yokomukaichō Gōsen-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Inari|ja|稲荷神|12=draft}} & a '''pair of white foxes''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Immortal riding a yellow crane'''
=== Second Float: Shōjō Yama (猩猩山) === * '''Ogawachō Giyū-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Shōjō|ja|猩猩|12=draft}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Phoenix carrying an immortal'''
=== Third Float: Daikoku Yama (大黒山) === * '''Yanagimachi Kyōshin-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Daikokuten|ja|大黒天|12=draft}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Yoshimitsu receiving a secret tune'''
=== Fourth Float: Ebisu Yama (蛭子山) === * '''Honmachi & Tanabu-chō Myōsei-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Ebisu|ja|蛭子|12=draft}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Great turtle carrying an immortal'''
=== Final Float: Kōro Hō (香爐峯) === * '''Shinmachi Shinsei-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Sei Shōnagon|ja|清少納言|12=draft}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Oniwakamaru riding a koi fish'''
== Festival Music and Chants == The music features '''flutes, drums, gongs, and taiko'''. Two major musical styles: * '''Gion-bayashi''' (*Gion Music*): Played '''during daytime''', characterized by '''a melancholic, elegant melody'''. * '''Yamaya-re''' (*Nighttime music*): A '''fast-paced, elaborate flute melody''' played at night.
Different '''chants''' are used for float turns: * '''Gōsen-gumi (Inari Yama):''' "Todoro-tōnō" * '''Giyū-gumi (Shōjō Yama):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō" * '''Kyōshin-gumi (Daikoku Yama):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō" * '''Myōsei-gumi (Ebisu Yama):''' "Todoro-se-nō" * '''Shinsei-gumi (Kōro Hō):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō"
== Schedule == Held annually '''August 18-20'''.
== External Links == - [http://www.city.mutsu.lg.jp/ Tanabu Shrine Grand Festival – Mutsu City Official Website]

* The festival was '''originally held on the lunar calendar dates of July 18-20''', but in '''1870 (Meiji 3),''' it was '''changed to the current dates of August 18-20'''.
== Festival Floats (Yama) == The '''five districts of Tanabu''' (*Tanabu 5-chō*: '''Yokomukaichō, Ogawachō, Yanagimachi, Honmachi, and Shinmachi''') each have '''a traditional wooden festival float''', known as '''"Yama"''' (山車). These floats are '''black-lacquered, two-story structures'''.
- The '''lower level''' houses the '''musicians''' (*Noriko*, 囃子方). - The '''upper level''' carries '''the enshrined deity''' (*Goshintai*, 御神体). - Each '''Yama''' has '''unique carvings, lacquer paintings, roof styles, and decorative metal fittings'''. - The '''wheels are wooden''', and '''turning corners''' is difficult due to the '''height''' of the Yama, requiring '''skilled handlers (Teko, 梃子)''' to rotate the floats at intersections. * '''Thick ropes''' are attached to the '''Yama''', and they are '''pulled by hand'''.
The '''decorations change between day and night''': * '''Daytime:''' The '''Yama''' is adorned with '''embroidered curtains''' (*Ōmizuhiki-maku, Komizuhiki-maku, Mikaeri-maku, and Misu*). These textiles were traditionally '''ordered from Kyoto''' or '''imported from China via Nagasaki'''. * '''Nighttime:''' The curtains are replaced with '''illuminated painted panels (Gaku, 額)''', drawn by '''local artists''' and '''redesigned every year'''.
The festival '''groups (Kumi, 組)''' manage the '''Yama operation''', following a '''strict hierarchy''': * '''Kumi Leader (組頭, Kumitō)''' * '''Vice Leader (副組頭)''' * '''Sub-leaders (小頭, Kojirō)''' * '''Councilors (理事, Rijī)''' * '''Executive members (幹事, Kanjī)''' - Members '''"retire" at around age 40'''.
== List of Floats == === Lead Float: Inari Yama (稲荷山) === * '''Yokomukaichō Gōsen-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Inari|ja|稲荷神|12=draft}} & a '''pair of white foxes''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Immortal riding a yellow crane'''
=== Second Float: Shōjō Yama (猩猩山) === * '''Ogawachō Giyū-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Shōjō|ja|猩猩|12=draft}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Phoenix carrying an immortal'''
=== Third Float: Daikoku Yama (大黒山) === * '''Yanagimachi Kyōshin-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Daikokuten|ja|大黒天|12=draft}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Yoshimitsu receiving a secret tune'''
=== Fourth Float: Ebisu Yama (蛭子山) === * '''Honmachi & Tanabu-chō Myōsei-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Ebisu|ja|蛭子|12=draft}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Great turtle carrying an immortal'''
=== Final Float: Kōro Hō (香爐峯) === * '''Shinmachi Shinsei-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Sei Shōnagon|ja|清少納言|12=draft}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Oniwakamaru riding a koi fish'''
== Festival Music and Chants == The music features '''flutes, drums, gongs, and taiko'''. Two major musical styles: * '''Gion-bayashi''' (*Gion Music*): Played '''during daytime''', characterized by '''a melancholic, elegant melody'''. * '''Yamaya-re''' (*Nighttime music*): A '''fast-paced, elaborate flute melody''' played at night.
Different '''chants''' are used for float turns: * '''Gōsen-gumi (Inari Yama):''' "Todoro-tōnō" * '''Giyū-gumi (Shōjō Yama):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō" * '''Kyōshin-gumi (Daikoku Yama):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō" * '''Myōsei-gumi (Ebisu Yama):''' "Todoro-se-nō" * '''Shinsei-gumi (Kōro Hō):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō"
== Schedule == Held annually '''August 18-20'''.
== External Links == - [http://www.city.mutsu.lg.jp/ Tanabu Shrine Grand Festival – Mutsu City Official Website]
<!--Tanabu Festival-->

revision.text:
[[File:Aomori- Tanabu Festival-m.jpg|thumb|270px|Tanabu Festival]] The '''Tanabu Festival''' (田名部まつり, *Tanabu Matsuri*) is the '''annual grand festival''' (*Reitaisai*) of '''Tanabu Shrine''' in '''{{ill|Mutsu, Aomori|ja|むつ市|12=draft}}'''. It has been designated as an '''intangible folk cultural property''' (*Mukei Minzoku Bunkazai*) of '''{{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県|12=draft}}'''.
== History and Background == * The '''origin''' of the '''Tanabu Festival''' is '''uncertain''', but it is said to have a '''history of over 370 years'''{{fact|date=August 2023}}.
* According to '''"Maki no Asatsuyu"''', written by the '''Edo-period traveler {{ill|Sugao Masumi|ja|菅江真澄|12=draft}}''' during his visit to Tanabu, the festival was already being held in a '''form similar to the present-day event by 1793''' (*Kansei 5*).
* '''Tanabu''' once housed the '''daikansho (magistrate's office)''' of the '''{{ill|Nanbu Domain|ja|南部藩|12=draft}}''', making it the '''center of industry, economy, and culture''' in '''Shimokita'''. Although Tanabu itself did not have a '''seaport''', goods from '''{{ill|Kitamaebune|ja|北前船|12=draft}}''' ships docked in '''{{ill|Mutsu Bay|ja|陸奥湾|12=draft}}''' (*Ōminato Bay*), were transported '''via the Tanabu River''', and helped the town flourish.
* Shimokita exported '''Hiba cypress, abalone, sea cucumber, and kelp''' to the Kansai region, while rare goods arrived from various parts of Japan via the '''West-bound shipping route''' (*Saikairi Kairō*). Along with these goods, '''language and culture''' were also introduced to the region.
* Shimokita’s '''products were highly valued''' as '''luxury goods''' and sold as '''Nagasaki Tawaramono''' (*high-end exports to China*). This trade brought '''wealth''' to the townspeople. Though Shimokita was sometimes called a '''"barren land"''', the people could '''simply buy''' food when they couldn't grow their own. It is even said that the '''people of Shimokita did not struggle to eat''' and had the leisure to '''enjoy poetry, calligraphy, and education'''.
* Since the '''early Edo period''', many '''{{ill|Ōmi merchants|ja|近江Merchant|12=draft}}''' were active in Shimokita, playing a major role in '''spreading language and culture'''. It is believed that through these merchants—who came from present-day '''Shiga Prefecture'''—the '''traditions of Kyoto's {{ill|Gion Festival|ja|祇園祭|12=draft}}''' were introduced to Tanabu. This influence is evident in the '''festival floats (Yama)''' and '''festival music''', earning Tanabu Festival the nickname '''"Miyabi of the North"''' (*北のみやび*).
* The '''night festival music (Yamaya-re)''' resembles the '''Chichibu Night Festival''' and other '''Kantō-style''' festival melodies, suggesting influences from regions beyond Kyoto and Shiga.
* The '''daytime decorations''' reflect the '''extravagance of Kyoto’s Gion Festival''', with some items made '''in Kyoto''' or even '''imported from China and Persia'''. Meanwhile, the '''nighttime decorations''', such as the '''Mikaeri-e (rear-facing paintings)''', are influenced by '''Nebuta festivals''' from '''Tsugaru and Shimokita''' and are crafted locally.
* The origins of the '''tradition of Tanabu Festival''' remain unclear, but one thing is certain: without the '''economic power of the townspeople''', they could not have afforded '''lavish festival floats, ornate furnishings, and sacred objects'''. Likewise, without '''community spirit and pride''', they could not have '''maintained and passed down''' these traditions.
* The festival was '''originally held on the lunar calendar dates of July 18-20''', but in '''1870 (Meiji 3),''' it was '''changed to the current dates of August 18-20'''.
== Festival Floats (Yama) == The '''five districts of Tanabu''' (*Tanabu 5-chō*: '''Yokomukaichō, Ogawachō, Yanagimachi, Honmachi, and Shinmachi''') each have '''a traditional wooden festival float''', known as '''"Yama"''' (山車). These floats are '''black-lacquered, two-story structures'''.
- The '''lower level''' houses the '''musicians''' (*Noriko*, 囃子方). - The '''upper level''' carries '''the enshrined deity''' (*Goshintai*, 御神体). - Each '''Yama''' has '''unique carvings, lacquer paintings, roof styles, and decorative metal fittings'''. - The '''wheels are wooden''', and '''turning corners''' is difficult due to the '''height''' of the Yama, requiring '''skilled handlers (Teko, 梃子)''' to rotate the floats at intersections. * '''Thick ropes''' are attached to the '''Yama''', and they are '''pulled by hand'''.
The '''decorations change between day and night''': * '''Daytime:''' The '''Yama''' is adorned with '''embroidered curtains''' (*Ōmizuhiki-maku, Komizuhiki-maku, Mikaeri-maku, and Misu*). These textiles were traditionally '''ordered from Kyoto''' or '''imported from China via Nagasaki'''. * '''Nighttime:''' The curtains are replaced with '''illuminated painted panels (Gaku, 額)''', drawn by '''local artists''' and '''redesigned every year'''.
The festival '''groups (Kumi, 組)''' manage the '''Yama operation''', following a '''strict hierarchy''': * '''Kumi Leader (組頭, Kumitō)''' * '''Vice Leader (副組頭)''' * '''Sub-leaders (小頭, Kojirō)''' * '''Councilors (理事, Rijī)''' * '''Executive members (幹事, Kanjī)''' - Members '''"retire" at around age 40'''.
== List of Floats == === Lead Float: Inari Yama (稲荷山) === * '''Yokomukaichō Gōsen-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Inari|ja|稲荷神|12=draft}} & a '''pair of white foxes''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Immortal riding a yellow crane'''
=== Second Float: Shōjō Yama (猩猩山) === * '''Ogawachō Giyū-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Shōjō|ja|猩猩|12=draft}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Phoenix carrying an immortal'''
=== Third Float: Daikoku Yama (大黒山) === * '''Yanagimachi Kyōshin-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Daikokuten|ja|大黒天|12=draft}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Yoshimitsu receiving a secret tune'''
=== Fourth Float: Ebisu Yama (蛭子山) === * '''Honmachi & Tanabu-chō Myōsei-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Ebisu|ja|蛭子|12=draft}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Great turtle carrying an immortal'''
=== Final Float: Kōro Hō (香爐峯) === * '''Shinmachi Shinsei-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Sei Shōnagon|ja|清少納言|12=draft}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Oniwakamaru riding a koi fish'''
== Festival Music and Chants == The music features '''flutes, drums, gongs, and taiko'''. Two major musical styles: * '''Gion-bayashi''' (*Gion Music*): Played '''during daytime''', characterized by '''a melancholic, elegant melody'''. * '''Yamaya-re''' (*Nighttime music*): A '''fast-paced, elaborate flute melody''' played at night.
Different '''chants''' are used for float turns: * '''Gōsen-gumi (Inari Yama):''' "Todoro-tōnō" * '''Giyū-gumi (Shōjō Yama):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō" * '''Kyōshin-gumi (Daikoku Yama):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō" * '''Myōsei-gumi (Ebisu Yama):''' "Todoro-se-nō" * '''Shinsei-gumi (Kōro Hō):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō"
== Schedule == Held annually '''August 18-20'''.
== External Links == - [http://www.city.mutsu.lg.jp/ Tanabu Shrine Grand Festival – Mutsu City Official Website]

== List of Floats == === Lead Float: Inari Yama (稲荷山) === * '''Yokomukaichō Gōsen-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Inari|ja|稲荷神|12=draft}} & a '''pair of white foxes''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Immortal riding a yellow crane'''
=== Second Float: Shōjō Yama (猩猩山) === * '''Ogawachō Giyū-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Shōjō|ja|猩猩|12=draft}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Phoenix carrying an immortal'''
=== Third Float: Daikoku Yama (大黒山) === * '''Yanagimachi Kyōshin-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Daikokuten|ja|大黒天|12=draft}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Yoshimitsu receiving a secret tune'''
=== Fourth Float: Ebisu Yama (蛭子山) === * '''Honmachi & Tanabu-chō Myōsei-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Ebisu|ja|蛭子|12=draft}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Great turtle carrying an immortal'''
=== Final Float: Kōro Hō (香爐峯) === * '''Shinmachi Shinsei-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Sei Shōnagon|ja|清少納言|12=draft}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Oniwakamaru riding a koi fish'''
== Festival Music and Chants == The music features '''flutes, drums, gongs, and taiko'''. Two major musical styles: * '''Gion-bayashi''' (*Gion Music*): Played '''during daytime''', characterized by '''a melancholic, elegant melody'''. * '''Yamaya-re''' (*Nighttime music*): A '''fast-paced, elaborate flute melody''' played at night.
Different '''chants''' are used for float turns: * '''Gōsen-gumi (Inari Yama):''' "Todoro-tōnō" * '''Giyū-gumi (Shōjō Yama):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō" * '''Kyōshin-gumi (Daikoku Yama):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō" * '''Myōsei-gumi (Ebisu Yama):''' "Todoro-se-nō" * '''Shinsei-gumi (Kōro Hō):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō"
== Schedule == Held annually '''August 18-20'''.
== External Links == - [http://www.city.mutsu.lg.jp/ Tanabu Shrine Grand Festival – Mutsu City Official Website]
<!--Tanabu Festival-->

revision.text:
[[File:Aomori- Tanabu Festival-m.jpg|thumb|270px|Tanabu Festival]] The '''Tanabu Festival''' (田名部まつり, *Tanabu Matsuri*) is the '''annual grand festival''' (*Reitaisai*) of '''Tanabu Shrine''' in '''{{ill|Mutsu, Aomori|ja|むつ市|12=draft|12=draft}}'''. It has been designated as an '''intangible folk cultural property''' (*Mukei Minzoku Bunkazai*) of '''{{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県|12=draft|12=draft}}'''.
== History and Background == * The '''origin''' of the '''Tanabu Festival''' is '''uncertain''', but it is said to have a '''history of over 370 years'''{{fact|date=August 2023}}.
* According to '''"Maki no Asatsuyu"''', written by the '''Edo-period traveler {{ill|Sugao Masumi|ja|菅江真澄|12=draft|12=draft}}''' during his visit to Tanabu, the festival was already being held in a '''form similar to the present-day event by 1793''' (*Kansei 5*).
* '''Tanabu''' once housed the '''daikansho (magistrate's office)''' of the '''{{ill|Nanbu Domain|ja|南部藩|12=draft|12=draft}}''', making it the '''center of industry, economy, and culture''' in '''Shimokita'''. Although Tanabu itself did not have a '''seaport''', goods from '''{{ill|Kitamaebune|ja|北前船|12=draft|12=draft}}''' ships docked in '''{{ill|Mutsu Bay|ja|陸奥湾|12=draft|12=draft}}''' (*Ōminato Bay*), were transported '''via the Tanabu River''', and helped the town flourish.
* Shimokita exported '''Hiba cypress, abalone, sea cucumber, and kelp''' to the Kansai region, while rare goods arrived from various parts of Japan via the '''West-bound shipping route''' (*Saikairi Kairō*). Along with these goods, '''language and culture''' were also introduced to the region.
* Shimokita’s '''products were highly valued''' as '''luxury goods''' and sold as '''Nagasaki Tawaramono''' (*high-end exports to China*). This trade brought '''wealth''' to the townspeople. Though Shimokita was sometimes called a '''"barren land"''', the people could '''simply buy''' food when they couldn't grow their own. It is even said that the '''people of Shimokita did not struggle to eat''' and had the leisure to '''enjoy poetry, calligraphy, and education'''.
* Since the '''early Edo period''', many '''{{ill|Ōmi merchants|ja|近江Merchant|12=draft|12=draft}}''' were active in Shimokita, playing a major role in '''spreading language and culture'''. It is believed that through these merchants—who came from present-day '''Shiga Prefecture'''—the '''traditions of Kyoto's {{ill|Gion Festival|ja|祇園祭|12=draft|12=draft}}''' were introduced to Tanabu. This influence is evident in the '''festival floats (Yama)''' and '''festival music''', earning Tanabu Festival the nickname '''"Miyabi of the North"''' (*北のみやび*).
* The '''night festival music (Yamaya-re)''' resembles the '''Chichibu Night Festival''' and other '''Kantō-style''' festival melodies, suggesting influences from regions beyond Kyoto and Shiga.
* The '''daytime decorations''' reflect the '''extravagance of Kyoto’s Gion Festival''', with some items made '''in Kyoto''' or even '''imported from China and Persia'''. Meanwhile, the '''nighttime decorations''', such as the '''Mikaeri-e (rear-facing paintings)''', are influenced by '''Nebuta festivals''' from '''Tsugaru and Shimokita''' and are crafted locally.
* The origins of the '''tradition of Tanabu Festival''' remain unclear, but one thing is certain: without the '''economic power of the townspeople''', they could not have afforded '''lavish festival floats, ornate furnishings, and sacred objects'''. Likewise, without '''community spirit and pride''', they could not have '''maintained and passed down''' these traditions.
* The festival was '''originally held on the lunar calendar dates of July 18-20''', but in '''1870 (Meiji 3),''' it was '''changed to the current dates of August 18-20'''.
== Festival Floats (Yama) == The '''five districts of Tanabu''' (*Tanabu 5-chō*: '''Yokomukaichō, Ogawachō, Yanagimachi, Honmachi, and Shinmachi''') each have '''a traditional wooden festival float''', known as '''"Yama"''' (山車). These floats are '''black-lacquered, two-story structures'''.
- The '''lower level''' houses the '''musicians''' (*Noriko*, 囃子方). - The '''upper level''' carries '''the enshrined deity''' (*Goshintai*, 御神体). - Each '''Yama''' has '''unique carvings, lacquer paintings, roof styles, and decorative metal fittings'''. - The '''wheels are wooden''', and '''turning corners''' is difficult due to the '''height''' of the Yama, requiring '''skilled handlers (Teko, 梃子)''' to rotate the floats at intersections. * '''Thick ropes''' are attached to the '''Yama''', and they are '''pulled by hand'''.
The '''decorations change between day and night''': * '''Daytime:''' The '''Yama''' is adorned with '''embroidered curtains''' (*Ōmizuhiki-maku, Komizuhiki-maku, Mikaeri-maku, and Misu*). These textiles were traditionally '''ordered from Kyoto''' or '''imported from China via Nagasaki'''. * '''Nighttime:''' The curtains are replaced with '''illuminated painted panels (Gaku, 額)''', drawn by '''local artists''' and '''redesigned every year'''.
The festival '''groups (Kumi, 組)''' manage the '''Yama operation''', following a '''strict hierarchy''': * '''Kumi Leader (組頭, Kumitō)''' * '''Vice Leader (副組頭)''' * '''Sub-leaders (小頭, Kojirō)''' * '''Councilors (理事, Rijī)''' * '''Executive members (幹事, Kanjī)''' - Members '''"retire" at around age 40'''.
== List of Floats == === Lead Float: Inari Yama (稲荷山) === * '''Yokomukaichō Gōsen-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Inari|ja|稲荷神|12=draft|12=draft}} & a '''pair of white foxes''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Immortal riding a yellow crane'''
=== Second Float: Shōjō Yama (猩猩山) === * '''Ogawachō Giyū-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Shōjō|ja|猩猩|12=draft|12=draft}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Phoenix carrying an immortal'''
=== Third Float: Daikoku Yama (大黒山) === * '''Yanagimachi Kyōshin-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Daikokuten|ja|大黒天|12=draft|12=draft}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Yoshimitsu receiving a secret tune'''
=== Fourth Float: Ebisu Yama (蛭子山) === * '''Honmachi & Tanabu-chō Myōsei-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Ebisu|ja|蛭子|12=draft|12=draft}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Great turtle carrying an immortal'''
=== Final Float: Kōro Hō (香爐峯) === * '''Shinmachi Shinsei-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Sei Shōnagon|ja|清少納言|12=draft|12=draft}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Oniwakamaru riding a koi fish'''
== Festival Music and Chants == The music features '''flutes, drums, gongs, and taiko'''. Two major musical styles: * '''Gion-bayashi''' (*Gion Music*): Played '''during daytime''', characterized by '''a melancholic, elegant melody'''. * '''Yamaya-re''' (*Nighttime music*): A '''fast-paced, elaborate flute melody''' played at night.
Different '''chants''' are used for float turns: * '''Gōsen-gumi (Inari Yama):''' "Todoro-tōnō" * '''Giyū-gumi (Shōjō Yama):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō" * '''Kyōshin-gumi (Daikoku Yama):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō" * '''Myōsei-gumi (Ebisu Yama):''' "Todoro-se-nō" * '''Shinsei-gumi (Kōro Hō):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō"
== Schedule == Held annually '''August 18-20'''.
== External Links == - [http://www.city.mutsu.lg.jp/ Tanabu Shrine Grand Festival – Mutsu City Official Website]

This influence is evident in the '''festival floats (Yama)''' and '''festival music''', earning Tanabu Festival the nickname '''"Miyabi of the North"''' (*北のみやび*).

Likewise, without '''community spirit and pride''', they could not have '''maintained and passed down''' these traditions.

The '''decorations change between day and night''': * '''Daytime:''' The '''Yama''' is adorned with '''embroidered curtains''' (*Ōmizuhiki-maku, Komizuhiki-maku, Mikaeri-maku, and Misu*). These textiles were traditionally '''ordered from Kyoto''' or '''imported from China via Nagasaki'''. * '''Nighttime:''' The curtains are replaced with '''illuminated painted panels (Gaku, 額)''', drawn by '''local artists''' and '''redesigned every year'''.
The festival '''groups (Kumi, 組)''' manage the '''Yama operation''', following a '''strict hierarchy''': * '''Kumi Leader (組頭, Kumitō)''' * '''Vice Leader (副組頭)''' * '''Sub-leaders (小頭, Kojirō)''' * '''Councilors (理事, Rijī)''' * '''Executive members (幹事, Kanjī)''' - Members '''"retire" at around age 40'''.

revision.comment: 81 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:田名部まつり up to rev 101873170
revision.text:
[[File:Aomori- Tanabu Festival-m.jpg|thumb|270px|田名部まつり]] '''田名部まつり'''（'''たなぶまつり'''）は[[青森県]][[むつ市]]にある田名部神社の例大祭である。[[青森県指定文化財一覧#民俗文化財|青森県]]の無形[[民俗文化財]]に指定されている。
== 歴史・背景 == *'''田名部まつり'''の起源がいつかは定かではないが、{{要出典範囲|date=2023年8月|{{いつ範囲|date=2023年8月|約370年以上の歴史をもつ}}}}といわれている。
*[[江戸時代]]中期の紀行家[[菅江真澄]]が田名部を訪れた際に書いた『牧の朝露』によれば、[[寛政]]5年（1793）にはすでに現在の運行形態に近い状態で行われていたことがわかる。
*かつて'''田名部'''は[[南部藩]]の[[代官所]]が置かれ、下北の産業・経済・文化の中心地となっていた。田名部自体には海に開けた港はなかったが、[[陸奥湾]]（[[大湊湾]]）に停泊した[[北前船]]からの荷の積み卸しが[[田名部川]]を通して行われ、その水運で栄えた町であった。下北からは上方に向けて[[ヒバ]]、[[アワビ]]、[[ナマコ]]、[[コンブ]]が出荷され、諸国からは[[西廻海運|西廻り航路]]を通って数々の珍しい物品がもたらされた。また、物だけではなく'''言葉'''や'''文化'''ももたらされた。
*下北の産物は高級品として扱われ、上方や中国に長崎俵物として売られていった。そうして下北の町人は富を得たのである。下北は'''不毛の地'''と称されることがたびたびあるが、地元で作物が収穫できなければ買えば良かったのである。実際、当時の下北人は食うに困らず、[[俳諧]]などの文学や書をたしなみ、学問をする余裕があったと伝えられている。
*[[江戸時代]]初期より、下北では多くの[[近江商人]]が活躍し、これらの人々が言葉や文化の伝導に一役かっていた。近江商人の出身地である滋賀県などを経由して、[[京都]][[八坂神社]]の[[祇園祭]]が伝わったと思われ、その流れを汲み、山車の形態、囃子にその痕跡が見てとれる。それゆえ「'''北のみやび'''」とも称される。 *一方、夜囃子のヤマヤレは、秩父夜祭など関東の祭囃子に似た軽快なものであり、京都や近江(滋賀県)などだけではなく、他の地方からの影響、交流が広くあった可能性をうかがわせる。 *昼の装飾は京都祇園祭の影響のある豪華なものであり、京都などに発注、中には遠く中国やペルシャなどの諸外国の材料や技術で作成されたものまでもある。夜の装飾は見返り絵を始めとして、津軽地方・下北地方のねぶたの影響も加わったもので、町内で作成しているものである。
*どのような謂われで伝えられ、定着したのかは不明であるが、いずれにせよ町人の経済力がなければ、山車本体、山車に使う豪華な調度品、御神体などを買うことはできなかったであろうし、心意気や祭をするだけの心の余裕がなければ維持し守り続けることもできなかったであろう。
*'''田名部まつり'''は、かつて陰暦7月18-20日に行われていたが、1870年（明治3年）より現在の8月18-20日となった。
== 山車 == '''田名部5町'''（横迎町、小川町、柳町、本町、新町）には'''ヤマ'''と呼ばれる木製・黒漆塗りの[[山車]]がある。ヤマは2階建てで、下の階には'''乗子（のりこ）'''と呼ばれる囃子方が乗り、上の階には御神体を乗せて運行する。金具装飾、漆絵、屋根の形式など細部の造りや装飾は各ヤマで異なる。
車輪は木製の4輪である。車輪が木製であるだけではなく、'''ヤマ'''そのものの高さもあるため、辻を曲がるのは難しく、そのため'''梃子(テコ)'''と呼ばれる専門者が辻での回転を巧みに操作する。
'''ヤマ'''には左右一対の綱がつけられ、人々が曳くようになっている。
'''ヤマ'''は昼夜で飾りを替える。昼間は刺繍を施した'''大水引き幕'''、'''小水引幕'''、'''見送り幕'''、'''御簾'''で飾られる。これらの幕は古くより京都の業者などの制作を依頼したり、中国から輸入したものを長崎経由で購入したものである。一方、夜間は昼間の幕に替わり、地元の描き手によって描かれた'''額（がく）'''と呼ばれる絵灯籠に付け替えられ、中に明かりをともす。夜間の額は毎年描き変えられる。
ヤマの運行は'''組'''が取り仕切る。組は男性のみで構成される。中学生～高校生で囃子方の乗子、その後は正式に組の一員となり、厳格な上下関係の元にしきたりを学んでいく。組は'''組頭'''（'''くみとう'''）を筆頭とし、'''副組頭'''、小頭、理事、'''幹事（評議員）'''、などが続く。組員はおよそ四十歳で定年となる。
=== 先山 稲荷山(いなりやま) === *横迎町豪川組（ごうせんぐみ） **御神体 [[稲荷神]]・白狐左右一対 ***見送り幕 黄鶴に乗った[[仙人]]の図
=== 二番山 猩猩山(しょうじょうやま)=== *小川町義勇組（ぎゆうぐみ） **御神体 [[猩猩|猩猩神]] ***見送り幕 [[鳳凰]]が仙人を乗せて飛ぶ図
=== 三番山 大黒山(だいこくやま) === *柳町共進組（きょうしんぐみ） **御神体 [[大黒天]] ***見送り幕 義光が秘曲を授ける図
=== 四番山 蛭子山(えびすやま) === *本町・田名部町明盛組（みょうせいぐみ） **御神体 [[蛭子|蛭子様]] ***見送り幕 大亀が仙人を乗せて泳ぐ図
=== 後山 香爐峯(こうろほう) === *新町新盛組（しんせいぐみ） **御神体 [[清少納言]] ***見送り幕 [[鬼若丸]]が鯉に乗っている図 紅毛唐人憩図（こうもうとうじんいこいのず）
== 囃子・かけ声 == 囃子は[[横笛]]、[[鼓]]、[[手平鉦]]、[[太鼓|大太鼓]]、小太鼓で演奏される。主な囃子は二種類ある。 *'''祇園囃子''' 昼間、町内を運行時に奏でられる。静かで哀愁のある曲調。 *'''ヤマヤレ''' 夜間、田名部神社前に整列時および運行時に奏でられる。
アップテンポで技巧を凝らした笛のメロディラインが特徴である。
辻回しの掛け声にも種類があり *豪川組稲荷山「'''トドコートーノー'''」
*義勇組猩猩山「'''ヤデコラーセーノー'''」
*共進組大黒山「'''ヤデコラーセーノー'''」
*明盛組蛭子山「'''トドコーセーノー'''」
*新盛組香爐峯「'''ヤデコラーセーノー'''」 のかけ声で辻を回す。「'''ヤッ トーレッ'''」でヤマの向きを微調整する。夜間には「'''ヤマヤレ ヤマヤレ'''」のかけ声でヤマを曳く。「[[田名部おしまこ|おしまこ]]」や各組特有の持ち歌を唄いながら曳くこともある。
== 主な行事日程 == 毎年8/18-8/20に行われる。
;8月18日 :午前 大神楽参社、山車参社（町内を運行してから参社） :夕方 能舞奉納（田名部神社神楽殿）、前々夜祭 :午後9時半 山車、大神楽、能舞退社
;8月19日 :午前 大神楽参社 :正午 山車参社 :午後 招魂祭 :夕方 前夜祭、能舞奉納（田名部神社神楽殿） :午後10時 山車、大神楽、能舞退社
;8月20日 :午前 大神楽、山車参社、参進、例祭 :正午 山車出発、御神輿渡御 :夕方 山車横迎町点燈、御神輿還御、還御祭、神楽奉納 :午後8時半 山車帰社 :午後10時半 大神楽、能舞、山車退社
8月18日には[[田名部おしまこ|おしまこ流し踊り]]、8月19日にはみこし祭りがある。
== 運行地域 == [[むつ市]]田名部地区。
== 関連項目 == *[[むつ市]] *[[下北弁]] *[[祇園祭]] *[[滋賀県]][[高島市]] *[[箭根森八幡宮例大祭]] *[[川内八幡宮例大祭]]
== 外部リンク == *[http://www.jomon.ne.jp/~sato/ 北のみやび 田名部まつり (音声あり)] *[http://www.city.mutsu.lg.jp/index.cfm/16,1331,32,html 田名部神社例大祭] - むつ市
{{DEFAULTSORT:たなふまつり}} [[Category:青森県の祭り]] [[Category:山車祭り]] [[Category:むつ市]] [[Category:都道府県指定無形民俗文化財]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[田名部まつり]] to [[Tanabu Festival]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
[[File:Aomori- Tanabu Festival-m.jpg|thumb|270px|田名部まつり]] '''田名部まつり'''（'''たなぶまつり'''）は[[青森県]][[むつ市]]にある田名部神社の例大祭である。[[青森県指定文化財一覧#民俗文化財|青森県]]の無形[[民俗文化財]]に指定されている。
== 歴史・背景 == *'''田名部まつり'''の起源がいつかは定かではないが、{{要出典範囲|date=2023年8月|{{いつ範囲|date=2023年8月|約370年以上の歴史をもつ}}}}といわれている。
*[[江戸時代]]中期の紀行家[[菅江真澄]]が田名部を訪れた際に書いた『牧の朝露』によれば、[[寛政]]5年（1793）にはすでに現在の運行形態に近い状態で行われていたことがわかる。
*かつて'''田名部'''は[[南部藩]]の[[代官所]]が置かれ、下北の産業・経済・文化の中心地となっていた。田名部自体には海に開けた港はなかったが、[[陸奥湾]]（[[大湊湾]]）に停泊した[[北前船]]からの荷の積み卸しが[[田名部川]]を通して行われ、その水運で栄えた町であった。下北からは上方に向けて[[ヒバ]]、[[アワビ]]、[[ナマコ]]、[[コンブ]]が出荷され、諸国からは[[西廻海運|西廻り航路]]を通って数々の珍しい物品がもたらされた。また、物だけではなく'''言葉'''や'''文化'''ももたらされた。
*下北の産物は高級品として扱われ、上方や中国に長崎俵物として売られていった。そうして下北の町人は富を得たのである。下北は'''不毛の地'''と称されることがたびたびあるが、地元で作物が収穫できなければ買えば良かったのである。実際、当時の下北人は食うに困らず、[[俳諧]]などの文学や書をたしなみ、学問をする余裕があったと伝えられている。
*[[江戸時代]]初期より、下北では多くの[[近江商人]]が活躍し、これらの人々が言葉や文化の伝導に一役かっていた。近江商人の出身地である滋賀県などを経由して、[[京都]][[八坂神社]]の[[祇園祭]]が伝わったと思われ、その流れを汲み、山車の形態、囃子にその痕跡が見てとれる。それゆえ「'''北のみやび'''」とも称される。 *一方、夜囃子のヤマヤレは、秩父夜祭など関東の祭囃子に似た軽快なものであり、京都や近江(滋賀県)などだけではなく、他の地方からの影響、交流が広くあった可能性をうかがわせる。 *昼の装飾は京都祇園祭の影響のある豪華なものであり、京都などに発注、中には遠く中国やペルシャなどの諸外国の材料や技術で作成されたものまでもある。夜の装飾は見返り絵を始めとして、津軽地方・下北地方のねぶたの影響も加わったもので、町内で作成しているものである。
*どのような謂われで伝えられ、定着したのかは不明であるが、いずれにせよ町人の経済力がなければ、山車本体、山車に使う豪華な調度品、御神体などを買うことはできなかったであろうし、心意気や祭をするだけの心の余裕がなければ維持し守り続けることもできなかったであろう。
*'''田名部まつり'''は、かつて陰暦7月18-20日に行われていたが、1870年（明治3年）より現在の8月18-20日となった。
== 山車 == '''田名部5町'''（横迎町、小川町、柳町、本町、新町）には'''ヤマ'''と呼ばれる木製・黒漆塗りの[[山車]]がある。ヤマは2階建てで、下の階には'''乗子（のりこ）'''と呼ばれる囃子方が乗り、上の階には御神体を乗せて運行する。金具装飾、漆絵、屋根の形式など細部の造りや装飾は各ヤマで異なる。
車輪は木製の4輪である。車輪が木製であるだけではなく、'''ヤマ'''そのものの高さもあるため、辻を曲がるのは難しく、そのため'''梃子(テコ)'''と呼ばれる専門者が辻での回転を巧みに操作する。
'''ヤマ'''には左右一対の綱がつけられ、人々が曳くようになっている。
'''ヤマ'''は昼夜で飾りを替える。昼間は刺繍を施した'''大水引き幕'''、'''小水引幕'''、'''見送り幕'''、'''御簾'''で飾られる。これらの幕は古くより京都の業者などの制作を依頼したり、中国から輸入したものを長崎経由で購入したものである。一方、夜間は昼間の幕に替わり、地元の描き手によって描かれた'''額（がく）'''と呼ばれる絵灯籠に付け替えられ、中に明かりをともす。夜間の額は毎年描き変えられる。
ヤマの運行は'''組'''が取り仕切る。組は男性のみで構成される。中学生～高校生で囃子方の乗子、その後は正式に組の一員となり、厳格な上下関係の元にしきたりを学んでいく。組は'''組頭'''（'''くみとう'''）を筆頭とし、'''副組頭'''、小頭、理事、'''幹事（評議員）'''、などが続く。組員はおよそ四十歳で定年となる。
=== 先山 稲荷山(いなりやま) === *横迎町豪川組（ごうせんぐみ） **御神体 [[稲荷神]]・白狐左右一対 ***見送り幕 黄鶴に乗った[[仙人]]の図
=== 二番山 猩猩山(しょうじょうやま)=== *小川町義勇組（ぎゆうぐみ） **御神体 [[猩猩|猩猩神]] ***見送り幕 [[鳳凰]]が仙人を乗せて飛ぶ図
=== 三番山 大黒山(だいこくやま) === *柳町共進組（きょうしんぐみ） **御神体 [[大黒天]] ***見送り幕 義光が秘曲を授ける図
=== 四番山 蛭子山(えびすやま) === *本町・田名部町明盛組（みょうせいぐみ） **御神体 [[蛭子|蛭子様]] ***見送り幕 大亀が仙人を乗せて泳ぐ図
=== 後山 香爐峯(こうろほう) === *新町新盛組（しんせいぐみ） **御神体 [[清少納言]] ***見送り幕 [[鬼若丸]]が鯉に乗っている図 紅毛唐人憩図（こうもうとうじんいこいのず）
== 囃子・かけ声 == 囃子は[[横笛]]、[[鼓]]、[[手平鉦]]、[[太鼓|大太鼓]]、小太鼓で演奏される。主な囃子は二種類ある。 *'''祇園囃子''' 昼間、町内を運行時に奏でられる。静かで哀愁のある曲調。 *'''ヤマヤレ''' 夜間、田名部神社前に整列時および運行時に奏でられる。
アップテンポで技巧を凝らした笛のメロディラインが特徴である。
辻回しの掛け声にも種類があり *豪川組稲荷山「'''トドコートーノー'''」
*義勇組猩猩山「'''ヤデコラーセーノー'''」
*共進組大黒山「'''ヤデコラーセーノー'''」
*明盛組蛭子山「'''トドコーセーノー'''」
*新盛組香爐峯「'''ヤデコラーセーノー'''」 のかけ声で辻を回す。「'''ヤッ トーレッ'''」でヤマの向きを微調整する。夜間には「'''ヤマヤレ ヤマヤレ'''」のかけ声でヤマを曳く。「[[田名部おしまこ|おしまこ]]」や各組特有の持ち歌を唄いながら曳くこともある。
== 主な行事日程 == 毎年8/18-8/20に行われる。
;8月18日 :午前 大神楽参社、山車参社（町内を運行してから参社） :夕方 能舞奉納（田名部神社神楽殿）、前々夜祭 :午後9時半 山車、大神楽、能舞退社
;8月19日 :午前 大神楽参社 :正午 山車参社 :午後 招魂祭 :夕方 前夜祭、能舞奉納（田名部神社神楽殿） :午後10時 山車、大神楽、能舞退社
;8月20日 :午前 大神楽、山車参社、参進、例祭 :正午 山車出発、御神輿渡御 :夕方 山車横迎町点燈、御神輿還御、還御祭、神楽奉納 :午後8時半 山車帰社 :午後10時半 大神楽、能舞、山車退社
8月18日には[[田名部おしまこ|おしまこ流し踊り]]、8月19日にはみこし祭りがある。
== 運行地域 == [[むつ市]]田名部地区。
== 関連項目 == *[[むつ市]] *[[下北弁]] *[[祇園祭]] *[[滋賀県]][[高島市]] *[[箭根森八幡宮例大祭]] *[[川内八幡宮例大祭]]
== 外部リンク == *[http://www.jomon.ne.jp/~sato/ 北のみやび 田名部まつり (音声あり)] *[http://www.city.mutsu.lg.jp/index.cfm/16,1331,32,html 田名部神社例大祭] - むつ市

[[File:Aomori- Tanabu Festival-m.jpg|thumb|270px|Tanabu Festival]] The '''Tanabu Festival''' (田名部まつり, *Tanabu Matsuri*) is the '''annual grand festival''' (*Reitaisai*) of '''Tanabu Shrine''' in '''{{ill|Mutsu, Aomori|ja|むつ市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'''. It has been designated as an '''intangible folk cultural property''' (*Mukei Minzoku Bunkazai*) of '''{{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'''.
== History and Background == * The '''origin''' of the '''Tanabu Festival''' is '''uncertain''', but it is said to have a '''history of over 370 years'''{{fact|date=August 2023}}.
* According to '''"Maki no Asatsuyu"''', written by the '''Edo-period traveler {{ill|Sugao Masumi|ja|菅江真澄|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' during his visit to Tanabu, the festival was already being held in a '''form similar to the present-day event by 1793''' (*Kansei 5*).
* '''Tanabu''' once housed the '''daikansho (magistrate's office)''' of the '''{{ill|Nanbu Domain|ja|南部藩|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''', making it the '''center of industry, economy, and culture''' in '''Shimokita'''. Although Tanabu itself did not have a '''seaport''', goods from '''{{ill|Kitamaebune|ja|北前船|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' ships docked in '''{{ill|Mutsu Bay|ja|陸奥湾|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (*Ōminato Bay*), were transported '''via the Tanabu River''', and helped the town flourish.
* Shimokita exported '''Hiba cypress, abalone, sea cucumber, and kelp''' to the Kansai region, while rare goods arrived from various parts of Japan via the '''West-bound shipping route''' (*Saikairi Kairō*). Along with these goods, '''language and culture''' were also introduced to the region.
* Shimokita’s '''products were highly valued''' as '''luxury goods''' and sold as '''Nagasaki Tawaramono''' (*high-end exports to China*). This trade brought '''wealth''' to the townspeople. Though Shimokita was sometimes called a '''"barren land"''', the people could '''simply buy''' food when they couldn't grow their own. It is even said that the '''people of Shimokita did not struggle to eat''' and had the leisure to '''enjoy poetry, calligraphy, and education'''.
* Since the '''early Edo period''', many '''{{ill|Ōmi merchants|ja|近江Merchant|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' were active in Shimokita, playing a major role in '''spreading language and culture'''. It is believed that through these merchants—who came from present-day '''Shiga Prefecture'''—the '''traditions of Kyoto's {{ill|Gion Festival|ja|祇園祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' were introduced to Tanabu. This influence is evident in the '''festival floats (Yama)''' and '''festival music''', earning Tanabu Festival the nickname '''"Miyabi of the North"''' (*北のみやび*).
* The '''night festival music (Yamaya-re)''' resembles the '''Chichibu Night Festival''' and other '''Kantō-style''' festival melodies, suggesting influences from regions beyond Kyoto and Shiga.
* The '''daytime decorations''' reflect the '''extravagance of Kyoto’s Gion Festival''', with some items made '''in Kyoto''' or even '''imported from China and Persia'''. Meanwhile, the '''nighttime decorations''', such as the '''Mikaeri-e (rear-facing paintings)''', are influenced by '''Nebuta festivals''' from '''Tsugaru and Shimokita''' and are crafted locally.
* The origins of the '''tradition of Tanabu Festival''' remain unclear, but one thing is certain: without the '''economic power of the townspeople''', they could not have afforded '''lavish festival floats, ornate furnishings, and sacred objects'''. Likewise, without '''community spirit and pride''', they could not have '''maintained and passed down''' these traditions.
* The festival was '''originally held on the lunar calendar dates of July 18-20''', but in '''1870 (Meiji 3),''' it was '''changed to the current dates of August 18-20'''.
== Festival Floats (Yama) == The '''five districts of Tanabu''' (*Tanabu 5-chō*: '''Yokomukaichō, Ogawachō, Yanagimachi, Honmachi, and Shinmachi''') each have '''a traditional wooden festival float''', known as '''"Yama"''' (山車). These floats are '''black-lacquered, two-story structures'''.
- The '''lower level''' houses the '''musicians''' (*Noriko*, 囃子方). - The '''upper level''' carries '''the enshrined deity''' (*Goshintai*, 御神体). - Each '''Yama''' has '''unique carvings, lacquer paintings, roof styles, and decorative metal fittings'''. - The '''wheels are wooden''', and '''turning corners''' is difficult due to the '''height''' of the Yama, requiring '''skilled handlers (Teko, 梃子)''' to rotate the floats at intersections. * '''Thick ropes''' are attached to the '''Yama''', and they are '''pulled by hand'''.
The '''decorations change between day and night''': * '''Daytime:''' The '''Yama''' is adorned with '''embroidered curtains''' (*Ōmizuhiki-maku, Komizuhiki-maku, Mikaeri-maku, and Misu*). These textiles were traditionally '''ordered from Kyoto''' or '''imported from China via Nagasaki'''. * '''Nighttime:''' The curtains are replaced with '''illuminated painted panels (Gaku, 額)''', drawn by '''local artists''' and '''redesigned every year'''.
The festival '''groups (Kumi, 組)''' manage the '''Yama operation''', following a '''strict hierarchy''': * '''Kumi Leader (組頭, Kumitō)''' * '''Vice Leader (副組頭)''' * '''Sub-leaders (小頭, Kojirō)''' * '''Councilors (理事, Rijī)''' * '''Executive members (幹事, Kanjī)''' - Members '''"retire" at around age 40'''.
== List of Floats == === Lead Float: Inari Yama (稲荷山) === * '''Yokomukaichō Gōsen-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Inari|ja|稲荷神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} & a '''pair of white foxes''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Immortal riding a yellow crane'''
=== Second Float: Shōjō Yama (猩猩山) === * '''Ogawachō Giyū-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Shōjō|ja|猩猩|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Phoenix carrying an immortal'''
=== Third Float: Daikoku Yama (大黒山) === * '''Yanagimachi Kyōshin-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Daikokuten|ja|大黒天|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Yoshimitsu receiving a secret tune'''
=== Fourth Float: Ebisu Yama (蛭子山) === * '''Honmachi & Tanabu-chō Myōsei-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Ebisu|ja|蛭子|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Great turtle carrying an immortal'''
=== Final Float: Kōro Hō (香爐峯) === * '''Shinmachi Shinsei-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Sei Shōnagon|ja|清少納言|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Oniwakamaru riding a koi fish'''
== Festival Music and Chants == The music features '''flutes, drums, gongs, and taiko'''. Two major musical styles: * '''Gion-bayashi''' (*Gion Music*): Played '''during daytime''', characterized by '''a melancholic, elegant melody'''. * '''Yamaya-re''' (*Nighttime music*): A '''fast-paced, elaborate flute melody''' played at night.
Different '''chants''' are used for float turns: * '''Gōsen-gumi (Inari Yama):''' "Todoro-tōnō" * '''Giyū-gumi (Shōjō Yama):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō" * '''Kyōshin-gumi (Daikoku Yama):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō" * '''Myōsei-gumi (Ebisu Yama):''' "Todoro-se-nō" * '''Shinsei-gumi (Kōro Hō):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō"
== Schedule == Held annually '''August 18-20'''.
== External Links == - [http://www.city.mutsu.lg.jp/ Tanabu Shrine Grand Festival – Mutsu City Official Website]

<!--Tanabu Festival--> {{translated page|ja|田名部まつり|version=101873170|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[File:Aomori- Tanabu Festival-m.jpg|thumb|270px|Tanabu Festival]] The '''Tanabu Festival''' (田名部まつり, *Tanabu Matsuri*) is the '''annual grand festival''' (*Reitaisai*) of '''Tanabu Shrine''' in '''{{ill|Mutsu, Aomori|ja|むつ市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'''. It has been designated as an '''intangible folk cultural property''' (*Mukei Minzoku Bunkazai*) of '''{{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'''.
== History and Background == * The '''origin''' of the '''Tanabu Festival''' is '''uncertain''', but it is said to have a '''history of over 370 years'''{{fact|date=August 2023}}.
* According to '''"Maki no Asatsuyu"''', written by the '''Edo-period traveler {{ill|Sugao Masumi|ja|菅江真澄|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' during his visit to Tanabu, the festival was already being held in a '''form similar to the present-day event by 1793''' (*Kansei 5*).
* '''Tanabu''' once housed the '''daikansho (magistrate's office)''' of the '''{{ill|Nanbu Domain|ja|南部藩|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''', making it the '''center of industry, economy, and culture''' in '''Shimokita'''. Although Tanabu itself did not have a '''seaport''', goods from '''{{ill|Kitamaebune|ja|北前船|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' ships docked in '''{{ill|Mutsu Bay|ja|陸奥湾|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (*Ōminato Bay*), were transported '''via the Tanabu River''', and helped the town flourish.
* Shimokita exported '''Hiba cypress, abalone, sea cucumber, and kelp''' to the Kansai region, while rare goods arrived from various parts of Japan via the '''West-bound shipping route''' (*Saikairi Kairō*). Along with these goods, '''language and culture''' were also introduced to the region.
* Shimokita’s '''products were highly valued''' as '''luxury goods''' and sold as '''Nagasaki Tawaramono''' (*high-end exports to China*). This trade brought '''wealth''' to the townspeople. Though Shimokita was sometimes called a '''"barren land"''', the people could '''simply buy''' food when they couldn't grow their own. It is even said that the '''people of Shimokita did not struggle to eat''' and had the leisure to '''enjoy poetry, calligraphy, and education'''.
* Since the '''early Edo period''', many '''{{ill|Ōmi merchants|ja|近江Merchant|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' were active in Shimokita, playing a major role in '''spreading language and culture'''. It is believed that through these merchants—who came from present-day '''Shiga Prefecture'''—the '''traditions of Kyoto's {{ill|Gion Festival|ja|祇園祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' were introduced to Tanabu. This influence is evident in the '''festival floats (Yama)''' and '''festival music''', earning Tanabu Festival the nickname '''"Miyabi of the North"''' (*北のみやび*).
* The '''night festival music (Yamaya-re)''' resembles the '''Chichibu Night Festival''' and other '''Kantō-style''' festival melodies, suggesting influences from regions beyond Kyoto and Shiga.
* The '''daytime decorations''' reflect the '''extravagance of Kyoto’s Gion Festival''', with some items made '''in Kyoto''' or even '''imported from China and Persia'''. Meanwhile, the '''nighttime decorations''', such as the '''Mikaeri-e (rear-facing paintings)''', are influenced by '''Nebuta festivals''' from '''Tsugaru and Shimokita''' and are crafted locally.
* The origins of the '''tradition of Tanabu Festival''' remain unclear, but one thing is certain: without the '''economic power of the townspeople''', they could not have afforded '''lavish festival floats, ornate furnishings, and sacred objects'''. Likewise, without '''community spirit and pride''', they could not have '''maintained and passed down''' these traditions.
* The festival was '''originally held on the lunar calendar dates of July 18-20''', but in '''1870 (Meiji 3),''' it was '''changed to the current dates of August 18-20'''.
== Festival Floats (Yama) == The '''five districts of Tanabu''' (*Tanabu 5-chō*: '''Yokomukaichō, Ogawachō, Yanagimachi, Honmachi, and Shinmachi''') each have '''a traditional wooden festival float''', known as '''"Yama"''' (山車). These floats are '''black-lacquered, two-story structures'''.
- The '''lower level''' houses the '''musicians''' (*Noriko*, 囃子方). - The '''upper level''' carries '''the enshrined deity''' (*Goshintai*, 御神体). - Each '''Yama''' has '''unique carvings, lacquer paintings, roof styles, and decorative metal fittings'''. - The '''wheels are wooden''', and '''turning corners''' is difficult due to the '''height''' of the Yama, requiring '''skilled handlers (Teko, 梃子)''' to rotate the floats at intersections. * '''Thick ropes''' are attached to the '''Yama''', and they are '''pulled by hand'''.
The '''decorations change between day and night''': * '''Daytime:''' The '''Yama''' is adorned with '''embroidered curtains''' (*Ōmizuhiki-maku, Komizuhiki-maku, Mikaeri-maku, and Misu*). These textiles were traditionally '''ordered from Kyoto''' or '''imported from China via Nagasaki'''. * '''Nighttime:''' The curtains are replaced with '''illuminated painted panels (Gaku, 額)''', drawn by '''local artists''' and '''redesigned every year'''.
The festival '''groups (Kumi, 組)''' manage the '''Yama operation''', following a '''strict hierarchy''': * '''Kumi Leader (組頭, Kumitō)''' * '''Vice Leader (副組頭)''' * '''Sub-leaders (小頭, Kojirō)''' * '''Councilors (理事, Rijī)''' * '''Executive members (幹事, Kanjī)''' - Members '''"retire" at around age 40'''.
== List of Floats == === Lead Float: Inari Yama (稲荷山) === * '''Yokomukaichō Gōsen-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Inari|ja|稲荷神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} & a '''pair of white foxes''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Immortal riding a yellow crane'''
=== Second Float: Shōjō Yama (猩猩山) === * '''Ogawachō Giyū-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Shōjō|ja|猩猩|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Phoenix carrying an immortal'''
=== Third Float: Daikoku Yama (大黒山) === * '''Yanagimachi Kyōshin-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Daikokuten|ja|大黒天|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Yoshimitsu receiving a secret tune'''
=== Fourth Float: Ebisu Yama (蛭子山) === * '''Honmachi & Tanabu-chō Myōsei-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Ebisu|ja|蛭子|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Great turtle carrying an immortal'''
=== Final Float: Kōro Hō (香爐峯) === * '''Shinmachi Shinsei-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Sei Shōnagon|ja|清少納言|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Oniwakamaru riding a koi fish'''
== Festival Music and Chants == The music features '''flutes, drums, gongs, and taiko'''. Two major musical styles: * '''Gion-bayashi''' (*Gion Music*): Played '''during daytime''', characterized by '''a melancholic, elegant melody'''. * '''Yamaya-re''' (*Nighttime music*): A '''fast-paced, elaborate flute melody''' played at night.
Different '''chants''' are used for float turns: * '''Gōsen-gumi (Inari Yama):''' "Todoro-tōnō" * '''Giyū-gumi (Shōjō Yama):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō" * '''Kyōshin-gumi (Daikoku Yama):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō" * '''Myōsei-gumi (Ebisu Yama):''' "Todoro-se-nō" * '''Shinsei-gumi (Kōro Hō):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō"
== Schedule == Held annually '''August 18-20'''.
== External Links == - [http://www.city.mutsu.lg.jp/ Tanabu Shrine Grand Festival – Mutsu City Official Website]
