* Shimokita exported '''Hiba cypress, abalone, sea cucumber, and kelp''' to the Kansai region, while rare goods arrived from various parts of Japan via the '''West-bound shipping route''' (*Saikairi Kairō*). Along with these goods, '''language and culture''' were also introduced to the region.
* Shimokita’s '''products were highly valued''' as '''luxury goods''' and sold as '''Nagasaki Tawaramono''' (*high-end exports to China*). This trade brought '''wealth''' to the townspeople. Though Shimokita was sometimes called a '''"barren land"''', the people could '''simply buy''' food when they couldn't grow their own. It is even said that the '''people of Shimokita did not struggle to eat''' and had the leisure to '''enjoy poetry, calligraphy, and education'''.
* Since the '''early Edo period''', many '''{{ill|Ōmi merchants|ja|近江Merchant|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' were active in Shimokita, playing a major role in '''spreading language and culture'''. It is believed that through these merchants—who came from present-day '''Shiga Prefecture'''—the '''traditions of Kyoto's {{ill|Gion Festival|ja|祇園祭|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' were introduced to Tanabu. This influence is evident in the '''festival floats (Yama)''' and '''festival music''', earning Tanabu Festival the nickname '''"Miyabi of the North"''' (*北のみやび*).
* The '''night festival music (Yamaya-re)''' resembles the '''Chichibu Night Festival''' and other '''Kantō-style''' festival melodies, suggesting influences from regions beyond Kyoto and Shiga.
* The '''daytime decorations''' reflect the '''extravagance of Kyoto’s Gion Festival''', with some items made '''in Kyoto''' or even '''imported from China and Persia'''. Meanwhile, the '''nighttime decorations''', such as the '''Mikaeri-e (rear-facing paintings)''', are influenced by '''Nebuta festivals''' from '''Tsugaru and Shimokita''' and are crafted locally.
* The origins of the '''tradition of Tanabu Festival''' remain unclear, but one thing is certain: without the '''economic power of the townspeople''', they could not have afforded '''lavish festival floats, ornate furnishings, and sacred objects'''. Likewise, without '''community spirit and pride''', they could not have '''maintained and passed down''' these traditions.
* The festival was '''originally held on the lunar calendar dates of July 18-20''', but in '''1870 (Meiji 3),''' it was '''changed to the current dates of August 18-20'''.
== Festival Floats (Yama) == The '''five districts of Tanabu''' (*Tanabu 5-chō*: '''Yokomukaichō, Ogawachō, Yanagimachi, Honmachi, and Shinmachi''') each have '''a traditional wooden festival float''', known as '''"Yama"''' (山車). These floats are '''black-lacquered, two-story structures'''.
- The '''lower level''' houses the '''musicians''' (*Noriko*, 囃子方). - The '''upper level''' carries '''the enshrined deity''' (*Goshintai*, 御神体). - Each '''Yama''' has '''unique carvings, lacquer paintings, roof styles, and decorative metal fittings'''. - The '''wheels are wooden''', and '''turning corners''' is difficult due to the '''height''' of the Yama, requiring '''skilled handlers (Teko, 梃子)''' to rotate the floats at intersections. * '''Thick ropes''' are attached to the '''Yama''', and they are '''pulled by hand'''.
The '''decorations change between day and night''': * '''Daytime:''' The '''Yama''' is adorned with '''embroidered curtains''' (*Ōmizuhiki-maku, Komizuhiki-maku, Mikaeri-maku, and Misu*). These textiles were traditionally '''ordered from Kyoto''' or '''imported from China via Nagasaki'''. * '''Nighttime:''' The curtains are replaced with '''illuminated painted panels (Gaku, 額)''', drawn by '''local artists''' and '''redesigned every year'''.
The festival '''groups (Kumi, 組)''' manage the '''Yama operation''', following a '''strict hierarchy''': * '''Kumi Leader (組頭, Kumitō)''' * '''Vice Leader (副組頭)''' * '''Sub-leaders (小頭, Kojirō)''' * '''Councilors (理事, Rijī)''' * '''Executive members (幹事, Kanjī)''' - Members '''"retire" at around age 40'''.
== List of Floats == === Lead Float: Inari Yama (稲荷山) === * '''Yokomukaichō Gōsen-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Inari|ja|稲荷神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} & a '''pair of white foxes''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Immortal riding a yellow crane'''
=== Second Float: Shōjō Yama (猩猩山) === * '''Ogawachō Giyū-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Shōjō|ja|猩猩|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Phoenix carrying an immortal'''
=== Third Float: Daikoku Yama (大黒山) === * '''Yanagimachi Kyōshin-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Daikokuten|ja|大黒天|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Yoshimitsu receiving a secret tune'''
=== Fourth Float: Ebisu Yama (蛭子山) === * '''Honmachi & Tanabu-chō Myōsei-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Ebisu|ja|蛭子|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Great turtle carrying an immortal'''
=== Final Float: Kōro Hō (香爐峯) === * '''Shinmachi Shinsei-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Sei Shōnagon|ja|清少納言|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Oniwakamaru riding a koi fish'''
== Festival Music and Chants == The music features '''flutes, drums, gongs, and taiko'''. Two major musical styles: * '''Gion-bayashi''' (*Gion Music*): Played '''during daytime''', characterized by '''a melancholic, elegant melody'''. * '''Yamaya-re''' (*Nighttime music*): A '''fast-paced, elaborate flute melody''' played at night.
Different '''chants''' are used for float turns: * '''Gōsen-gumi (Inari Yama):''' "Todoro-tōnō" * '''Giyū-gumi (Shōjō Yama):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō" * '''Kyōshin-gumi (Daikoku Yama):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō" * '''Myōsei-gumi (Ebisu Yama):''' "Todoro-se-nō" * '''Shinsei-gumi (Kōro Hō):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō"
== Schedule == Held annually '''August 18-20'''.
== External Links == - [http://www.city.mutsu.lg.jp/ Tanabu Shrine Grand Festival – Mutsu City Official Website]
{{translated page|ja|田名部まつり|version=101873170|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[File:Aomori- Tanabu Festival-m.jpg|thumb|270px|Tanabu Festival]] The '''Tanabu Festival''' (田名部まつり, *Tanabu Matsuri*) is the '''annual grand festival''' (*Reitaisai*) of '''Tanabu Shrine''' in '''{{ill|Mutsu, Aomori|ja|むつ市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mutsu, Aomori|qq=}}'''. It has been designated as an '''intangible folk cultural property''' (*Mukei Minzoku Bunkazai*) of '''{{ill|Aomori Prefecture|ja|青森県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aomori Prefecture|qq=}}'''.
== History and Background == * The '''origin''' of the '''Tanabu Festival''' is '''uncertain''', but it is said to have a '''history of over 370 years'''{{fact|date=August 2023}}.
* According to '''"Maki no Asatsuyu"''', written by the '''Edo-period traveler {{ill|Sugao Masumi|ja|菅江真澄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugao Masumi|qq=}}''' during his visit to Tanabu, the festival was already being held in a '''form similar to the present-day event by 1793''' (*Kansei 5*).
* '''Tanabu''' once housed the '''daikansho (magistrate's office)''' of the '''{{ill|Nanbu Domain|ja|南部藩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Domain|qq=}}''', making it the '''center of industry, economy, and culture''' in '''Shimokita'''. Although Tanabu itself did not have a '''seaport''', goods from '''{{ill|Kitamaebune|ja|北前船|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitamaebune|qq=}}''' ships docked in '''{{ill|Mutsu Bay|ja|陸奥湾|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mutsu Bay|qq=}}''' (*Ōminato Bay*), were transported '''via the Tanabu River''', and helped the town flourish.
* Shimokita exported '''Hiba cypress, abalone, sea cucumber, and kelp''' to the Kansai region, while rare goods arrived from various parts of Japan via the '''West-bound shipping route''' (*Saikairi Kairō*). Along with these goods, '''language and culture''' were also introduced to the region.
* Shimokita’s '''products were highly valued''' as '''luxury goods''' and sold as '''Nagasaki Tawaramono''' (*high-end exports to China*). This trade brought '''wealth''' to the townspeople. Though Shimokita was sometimes called a '''"barren land"''', the people could '''simply buy''' food when they couldn't grow their own. It is even said that the '''people of Shimokita did not struggle to eat''' and had the leisure to '''enjoy poetry, calligraphy, and education'''.
* Since the '''early Edo period''', many '''{{ill|Ōmi merchants|ja|近江Merchant|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi merchants|qq=}}''' were active in Shimokita, playing a major role in '''spreading language and culture'''. It is believed that through these merchants—who came from present-day '''Shiga Prefecture'''—the '''traditions of Kyoto's {{ill|Gion Festival|ja|祇園祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Festival|qq=}}''' were introduced to Tanabu. This influence is evident in the '''festival floats (Yama)''' and '''festival music''', earning Tanabu Festival the nickname '''"Miyabi of the North"''' (*北のみやび*).
* The '''night festival music (Yamaya-re)''' resembles the '''Chichibu Night Festival''' and other '''Kantō-style''' festival melodies, suggesting influences from regions beyond Kyoto and Shiga.
* The '''daytime decorations''' reflect the '''extravagance of Kyoto’s Gion Festival''', with some items made '''in Kyoto''' or even '''imported from China and Persia'''. Meanwhile, the '''nighttime decorations''', such as the '''Mikaeri-e (rear-facing paintings)''', are influenced by '''Nebuta festivals''' from '''Tsugaru and Shimokita''' and are crafted locally.
* The origins of the '''tradition of Tanabu Festival''' remain unclear, but one thing is certain: without the '''economic power of the townspeople''', they could not have afforded '''lavish festival floats, ornate furnishings, and sacred objects'''. Likewise, without '''community spirit and pride''', they could not have '''maintained and passed down''' these traditions.
* The festival was '''originally held on the lunar calendar dates of July 18-20''', but in '''1870 (Meiji 3),''' it was '''changed to the current dates of August 18-20'''.
== Festival Floats (Yama) == The '''five districts of Tanabu''' (*Tanabu 5-chō*: '''Yokomukaichō, Ogawachō, Yanagimachi, Honmachi, and Shinmachi''') each have '''a traditional wooden festival float''', known as '''"Yama"''' (山車). These floats are '''black-lacquered, two-story structures'''.
- The '''lower level''' houses the '''musicians''' (*Noriko*, 囃子方). - The '''upper level''' carries '''the enshrined deity''' (*Goshintai*, 御神体). - Each '''Yama''' has '''unique carvings, lacquer paintings, roof styles, and decorative metal fittings'''. - The '''wheels are wooden''', and '''turning corners''' is difficult due to the '''height''' of the Yama, requiring '''skilled handlers (Teko, 梃子)''' to rotate the floats at intersections. * '''Thick ropes''' are attached to the '''Yama''', and they are '''pulled by hand'''.
The '''decorations change between day and night''': * '''Daytime:''' The '''Yama''' is adorned with '''embroidered curtains''' (*Ōmizuhiki-maku, Komizuhiki-maku, Mikaeri-maku, and Misu*). These textiles were traditionally '''ordered from Kyoto''' or '''imported from China via Nagasaki'''. * '''Nighttime:''' The curtains are replaced with '''illuminated painted panels (Gaku, 額)''', drawn by '''local artists''' and '''redesigned every year'''.
The festival '''groups (Kumi, 組)''' manage the '''Yama operation''', following a '''strict hierarchy''': * '''Kumi Leader (組頭, Kumitō)''' * '''Vice Leader (副組頭)''' * '''Sub-leaders (小頭, Kojirō)''' * '''Councilors (理事, Rijī)''' * '''Executive members (幹事, Kanjī)''' - Members '''"retire" at around age 40'''.
== List of Floats == === Lead Float: Inari Yama (稲荷山) === * '''Yokomukaichō Gōsen-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Inari|ja|稲荷神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari|qq=}} & a '''pair of white foxes''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Immortal riding a yellow crane'''
=== Second Float: Shōjō Yama (猩猩山) === * '''Ogawachō Giyū-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Shōjō|ja|猩猩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōjō|qq=}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Phoenix carrying an immortal'''
=== Third Float: Daikoku Yama (大黒山) === * '''Yanagimachi Kyōshin-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Daikokuten|ja|大黒天|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daikokuten|qq=}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Yoshimitsu receiving a secret tune'''
=== Fourth Float: Ebisu Yama (蛭子山) === * '''Honmachi & Tanabu-chō Myōsei-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Ebisu|ja|蛭子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebisu|qq=}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Great turtle carrying an immortal'''
=== Final Float: Kōro Hō (香爐峯) === * '''Shinmachi Shinsei-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Sei Shōnagon|ja|清少納言|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sei Shōnagon|qq=}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Oniwakamaru riding a koi fish'''
== Festival Music and Chants == The music features '''flutes, drums, gongs, and taiko'''. Two major musical styles: * '''Gion-bayashi''' (*Gion Music*): Played '''during daytime''', characterized by '''a melancholic, elegant melody'''. * '''Yamaya-re''' (*Nighttime music*): A '''fast-paced, elaborate flute melody''' played at night.
Different '''chants''' are used for float turns: * '''Gōsen-gumi (Inari Yama):''' "Todoro-tōnō" * '''Giyū-gumi (Shōjō Yama):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō" * '''Kyōshin-gumi (Daikoku Yama):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō" * '''Myōsei-gumi (Ebisu Yama):''' "Todoro-se-nō" * '''Shinsei-gumi (Kōro Hō):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō"
== Schedule == Held annually '''August 18-20'''.
== External Links == - [http://www.city.mutsu.lg.jp/ Tanabu Shrine Grand Festival – Mutsu City Official Website]

* According to '''"Maki no Asatsuyu"''', written by the '''Edo-period traveler {{ill|Sugao Masumi|ja|菅江真澄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugao Masumi|qq=}}''' during his visit to Tanabu, the festival was already being held in a '''form similar to the present-day event by 1793''' (*Kansei 5*).
* '''Tanabu''' once housed the '''daikansho (magistrate's office)''' of the '''{{ill|Nanbu Domain|ja|南部藩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Domain|qq=}}''', making it the '''center of industry, economy, and culture''' in '''Shimokita'''. Although Tanabu itself did not have a '''seaport''', goods from '''{{ill|Kitamaebune|ja|北前船|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitamaebune|qq=}}''' ships docked in '''{{ill|Mutsu Bay|ja|陸奥湾|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mutsu Bay|qq=}}''' (*Ōminato Bay*), were transported '''via the Tanabu River''', and helped the town flourish.
* Shimokita exported '''Hiba cypress, abalone, sea cucumber, and kelp''' to the Kansai region, while rare goods arrived from various parts of Japan via the '''West-bound shipping route''' (*Saikairi Kairō*). Along with these goods, '''language and culture''' were also introduced to the region.
* Shimokita’s '''products were highly valued''' as '''luxury goods''' and sold as '''Nagasaki Tawaramono''' (*high-end exports to China*). This trade brought '''wealth''' to the townspeople. Though Shimokita was sometimes called a '''"barren land"''', the people could '''simply buy''' food when they couldn't grow their own. It is even said that the '''people of Shimokita did not struggle to eat''' and had the leisure to '''enjoy poetry, calligraphy, and education'''.
* Since the '''early Edo period''', many '''{{ill|Ōmi merchants|ja|近江Merchant|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi merchants|qq=}}''' were active in Shimokita, playing a major role in '''spreading language and culture'''. It is believed that through these merchants—who came from present-day '''Shiga Prefecture'''—the '''traditions of Kyoto's {{ill|Gion Matsuri|lt=Gion Matsuri|lt=Gion Festival|ja|祇園祭|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Festival|qq=}}''' were introduced to Tanabu. This influence is evident in the '''festival floats (Yama)''' and '''festival music''', earning Tanabu Festival the nickname '''"Miyabi of the North"''' (*北のみやび*).
* The '''night festival music (Yamaya-re)''' resembles the '''Chichibu Night Festival''' and other '''Kantō-style''' festival melodies, suggesting influences from regions beyond Kyoto and Shiga.
* The '''daytime decorations''' reflect the '''extravagance of Kyoto’s Gion Festival''', with some items made '''in Kyoto''' or even '''imported from China and Persia'''. Meanwhile, the '''nighttime decorations''', such as the '''Mikaeri-e (rear-facing paintings)''', are influenced by '''Nebuta festivals''' from '''Tsugaru and Shimokita''' and are crafted locally.
* The origins of the '''tradition of Tanabu Festival''' remain unclear, but one thing is certain: without the '''economic power of the townspeople''', they could not have afforded '''lavish festival floats, ornate furnishings, and sacred objects'''. Likewise, without '''community spirit and pride''', they could not have '''maintained and passed down''' these traditions.
* The festival was '''originally held on the lunar calendar dates of July 18-20''', but in '''1870 (Meiji 3),''' it was '''changed to the current dates of August 18-20'''.
== Festival Floats (Yama) == The '''five districts of Tanabu''' (*Tanabu 5-chō*: '''Yokomukaichō, Ogawachō, Yanagimachi, Honmachi, and Shinmachi''') each have '''a traditional wooden festival float''', known as '''"Yama"''' (山車). These floats are '''black-lacquered, two-story structures'''.
- The '''lower level''' houses the '''musicians''' (*Noriko*, 囃子方). - The '''upper level''' carries '''the enshrined deity''' (*Goshintai*, 御神体). - Each '''Yama''' has '''unique carvings, lacquer paintings, roof styles, and decorative metal fittings'''. - The '''wheels are wooden''', and '''turning corners''' is difficult due to the '''height''' of the Yama, requiring '''skilled handlers (Teko, 梃子)''' to rotate the floats at intersections. * '''Thick ropes''' are attached to the '''Yama''', and they are '''pulled by hand'''.
The '''decorations change between day and night''': * '''Daytime:''' The '''Yama''' is adorned with '''embroidered curtains''' (*Ōmizuhiki-maku, Komizuhiki-maku, Mikaeri-maku, and Misu*). These textiles were traditionally '''ordered from Kyoto''' or '''imported from China via Nagasaki'''. * '''Nighttime:''' The curtains are replaced with '''illuminated painted panels (Gaku, 額)''', drawn by '''local artists''' and '''redesigned every year'''.
The festival '''groups (Kumi, 組)''' manage the '''Yama operation''', following a '''strict hierarchy''': * '''Kumi Leader (組頭, Kumitō)''' * '''Vice Leader (副組頭)''' * '''Sub-leaders (小頭, Kojirō)''' * '''Councilors (理事, Rijī)''' * '''Executive members (幹事, Kanjī)''' - Members '''"retire" at around age 40'''.
== List of Floats == === Lead Float: Inari Yama (稲荷山) === * '''Yokomukaichō Gōsen-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Inari|ja|稲荷神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari|qq=}} & a '''pair of white foxes''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Immortal riding a yellow crane'''
=== Second Float: Shōjō Yama (猩猩山) === * '''Ogawachō Giyū-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Shōjō|ja|猩猩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōjō|qq=}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Phoenix carrying an immortal'''
=== Third Float: Daikoku Yama (大黒山) === * '''Yanagimachi Kyōshin-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Daikokuten|ja|大黒天|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daikokuten|qq=}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Yoshimitsu receiving a secret tune'''
=== Fourth Float: Ebisu Yama (蛭子山) === * '''Honmachi & Tanabu-chō Myōsei-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Ebisu|ja|蛭子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebisu|qq=}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Great turtle carrying an immortal'''
=== Final Float: Kōro Hō (香爐峯) === * '''Shinmachi Shinsei-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Sei Shōnagon|ja|清少納言|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sei Shōnagon|qq=}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Oniwakamaru riding a koi fish'''
== Festival Music and Chants == The music features '''flutes, drums, gongs, and taiko'''. Two major musical styles: * '''Gion-bayashi''' (*Gion Music*): Played '''during daytime''', characterized by '''a melancholic, elegant melody'''. * '''Yamaya-re''' (*Nighttime music*): A '''fast-paced, elaborate flute melody''' played at night.
Different '''chants''' are used for float turns: * '''Gōsen-gumi (Inari Yama):''' "Todoro-tōnō" * '''Giyū-gumi (Shōjō Yama):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō" * '''Kyōshin-gumi (Daikoku Yama):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō" * '''Myōsei-gumi (Ebisu Yama):''' "Todoro-se-nō" * '''Shinsei-gumi (Kōro Hō):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō"
== Schedule == Held annually '''August 18-20'''.
== External Links == - [http://www.city.mutsu.lg.jp/ Tanabu Shrine Grand Festival – Mutsu City Official Website]
{{translated page|ja|田名部まつり|version=101873170|comment=Imported full ja history}}

* The '''night festival music (Yamaya-re)''' resembles the '''Chichibu Night Festival''' and other '''Kantō-style''' festival melodies, suggesting influences from regions beyond Kyoto and Shiga.

* The origins of the '''tradition of Tanabu Festival''' remain unclear, but one thing is certain: without the '''economic power of the townspeople''', they could not have afforded '''lavish festival floats, ornate furnishings, and sacred objects'''. Likewise, without '''community spirit and pride''', they could not have '''maintained and passed down''' these traditions.

revision.text:
[[File:Aomori- Tanabu Festival-m.jpg|thumb|270px|Tanabu Festival]] The '''Tanabu Festival''' (田名部まつり, *Tanabu Matsuri*) is the '''annual grand festival''' (*Reitaisai*) of '''Tanabu Shrine''' in '''[[:en:Mutsu, Aomori|Mutsu, Aomori]]'''. It has been designated as an '''intangible folk cultural property''' (*Mukei Minzoku Bunkazai*) of '''[[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]]'''.
== History and Background == * The '''origin''' of the '''Tanabu Festival''' is '''uncertain''', but it is said to have a '''history of over 370 years'''{{fact|date=August 2023}}.
* According to '''"Maki no Asatsuyu"''', written by the '''Edo-period traveler {{ill|Sugao Masumi|ja|菅江真澄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugao Masumi|qq=}}''' during his visit to Tanabu, the festival was already being held in a '''form similar to the present-day event by 1793''' (*Kansei 5*).
* '''Tanabu''' once housed the '''daikansho (magistrate's office)''' of the '''{{ill|Nanbu Domain|ja|南部藩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Domain|qq=}}''', making it the '''center of industry, economy, and culture''' in '''Shimokita'''. Although Tanabu itself did not have a '''seaport''', goods from '''[[:en:Kitamaebune|Kitamaebune]]''' ships docked in '''[[:en:Mutsu Bay|Mutsu Bay]]''' (*Ōminato Bay*), were transported '''via the Tanabu River''', and helped the town flourish.
* Shimokita exported '''Hiba cypress, abalone, sea cucumber, and kelp''' to the Kansai region, while rare goods arrived from various parts of Japan via the '''West-bound shipping route''' (*Saikairi Kairō*). Along with these goods, '''language and culture''' were also introduced to the region.
* Shimokita’s '''products were highly valued''' as '''luxury goods''' and sold as '''Nagasaki Tawaramono''' (*high-end exports to China*). This trade brought '''wealth''' to the townspeople. Though Shimokita was sometimes called a '''"barren land"''', the people could '''simply buy''' food when they couldn't grow their own. It is even said that the '''people of Shimokita did not struggle to eat''' and had the leisure to '''enjoy poetry, calligraphy, and education'''.
* Since the '''early Edo period''', many '''{{ill|Ōmi merchants|ja|近江Merchant|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi merchants|qq=}}''' were active in Shimokita, playing a major role in '''spreading language and culture'''. It is believed that through these merchants—who came from present-day '''Shiga Prefecture'''—the '''traditions of Kyoto's [[:en:Gion Matsuri|Gion Festival]]''' were introduced to Tanabu. This influence is evident in the '''festival floats (Yama)''' and '''festival music''', earning Tanabu Festival the nickname '''"Miyabi of the North"''' (*北のみやび*).
* The '''night festival music (Yamaya-re)''' resembles the '''Chichibu Night Festival''' and other '''Kantō-style''' festival melodies, suggesting influences from regions beyond Kyoto and Shiga.
* The '''daytime decorations''' reflect the '''extravagance of Kyoto’s Gion Festival''', with some items made '''in Kyoto''' or even '''imported from China and Persia'''. Meanwhile, the '''nighttime decorations''', such as the '''Mikaeri-e (rear-facing paintings)''', are influenced by '''Nebuta festivals''' from '''Tsugaru and Shimokita''' and are crafted locally.
* The origins of the '''tradition of Tanabu Festival''' remain unclear, but one thing is certain: without the '''economic power of the townspeople''', they could not have afforded '''lavish festival floats, ornate furnishings, and sacred objects'''. Likewise, without '''community spirit and pride''', they could not have '''maintained and passed down''' these traditions.
* The festival was '''originally held on the lunar calendar dates of July 18-20''', but in '''1870 (Meiji 3),''' it was '''changed to the current dates of August 18-20'''.
== Festival Floats (Yama) == The '''five districts of Tanabu''' (*Tanabu 5-chō*: '''Yokomukaichō, Ogawachō, Yanagimachi, Honmachi, and Shinmachi''') each have '''a traditional wooden festival float''', known as '''"Yama"''' (山車). These floats are '''black-lacquered, two-story structures'''.
- The '''lower level''' houses the '''musicians''' (*Noriko*, 囃子方). - The '''upper level''' carries '''the enshrined deity''' (*Goshintai*, 御神体). - Each '''Yama''' has '''unique carvings, lacquer paintings, roof styles, and decorative metal fittings'''. - The '''wheels are wooden''', and '''turning corners''' is difficult due to the '''height''' of the Yama, requiring '''skilled handlers (Teko, 梃子)''' to rotate the floats at intersections. * '''Thick ropes''' are attached to the '''Yama''', and they are '''pulled by hand'''.
The '''decorations change between day and night''': * '''Daytime:''' The '''Yama''' is adorned with '''embroidered curtains''' (*Ōmizuhiki-maku, Komizuhiki-maku, Mikaeri-maku, and Misu*). These textiles were traditionally '''ordered from Kyoto''' or '''imported from China via Nagasaki'''. * '''Nighttime:''' The curtains are replaced with '''illuminated painted panels (Gaku, 額)''', drawn by '''local artists''' and '''redesigned every year'''.
The festival '''groups (Kumi, 組)''' manage the '''Yama operation''', following a '''strict hierarchy''': * '''Kumi Leader (組頭, Kumitō)''' * '''Vice Leader (副組頭)''' * '''Sub-leaders (小頭, Kojirō)''' * '''Councilors (理事, Rijī)''' * '''Executive members (幹事, Kanjī)''' - Members '''"retire" at around age 40'''.
== List of Floats == === Lead Float: Inari Yama (稲荷山) === * '''Yokomukaichō Gōsen-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''[[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]] & a '''pair of white foxes''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Immortal riding a yellow crane'''
=== Second Float: Shōjō Yama (猩猩山) === * '''Ogawachō Giyū-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''[[:en:Shōjō|Shōjō]]''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Phoenix carrying an immortal'''
=== Third Float: Daikoku Yama (大黒山) === * '''Yanagimachi Kyōshin-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''[[:en:Daikokuten|Daikokuten]]''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Yoshimitsu receiving a secret tune'''
=== Fourth Float: Ebisu Yama (蛭子山) === * '''Honmachi & Tanabu-chō Myōsei-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Ebisu|ja|蛭子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebisu|qq=}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Great turtle carrying an immortal'''
=== Final Float: Kōro Hō (香爐峯) === * '''Shinmachi Shinsei-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''[[:en:Sei Shōnagon|Sei Shōnagon]]''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Oniwakamaru riding a koi fish'''
== Festival Music and Chants == The music features '''flutes, drums, gongs, and taiko'''. Two major musical styles: * '''Gion-bayashi''' (*Gion Music*): Played '''during daytime''', characterized by '''a melancholic, elegant melody'''. * '''Yamaya-re''' (*Nighttime music*): A '''fast-paced, elaborate flute melody''' played at night.
Different '''chants''' are used for float turns: * '''Gōsen-gumi (Inari Yama):''' "Todoro-tōnō" * '''Giyū-gumi (Shōjō Yama):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō" * '''Kyōshin-gumi (Daikoku Yama):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō" * '''Myōsei-gumi (Ebisu Yama):''' "Todoro-se-nō" * '''Shinsei-gumi (Kōro Hō):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō"
== Schedule == Held annually '''August 18-20'''.
== External Links == - [http://www.city.mutsu.lg.jp/ Tanabu Shrine Grand Festival – Mutsu City Official Website]
{{translated page|ja|田名部まつり|version=101873170|comment=Imported full ja history}}

Though Shimokita was sometimes called a '''"barren land"''', the people could '''simply buy''' food when they couldn't grow their own. It is even said that the '''people of Shimokita did not struggle to eat''' and had the leisure to '''enjoy poetry, calligraphy, and education'''.
* Since the '''early Edo period''', many '''{{ill|Ōmi merchants|ja|近江Merchant|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi merchants|qq=}}''' were active in Shimokita, playing a major role in '''spreading language and culture'''. It is believed that through these merchants—who came from present-day '''Shiga Prefecture'''—the '''traditions of Kyoto's [[:en:Gion Matsuri|Gion Festival]]''' were introduced to Tanabu. This influence is evident in the '''festival floats (Yama)''' and '''festival music''', earning Tanabu Festival the nickname '''"Miyabi of the North"''' (*北のみやび*).
* The '''night festival music (Yamaya-re)''' resembles the '''Chichibu Night Festival''' and other '''Kantō-style''' festival melodies, suggesting influences from regions beyond Kyoto and Shiga.
* The '''daytime decorations''' reflect the '''extravagance of Kyoto’s Gion Festival''', with some items made '''in Kyoto''' or even '''imported from China and Persia'''. Meanwhile, the '''nighttime decorations''', such as the '''Mikaeri-e (rear-facing paintings)''', are influenced by '''Nebuta festivals''' from '''Tsugaru and Shimokita''' and are crafted locally.
* The origins of the '''tradition of Tanabu Festival''' remain unclear, but one thing is certain: without the '''economic power of the townspeople''', they could not have afforded '''lavish festival floats, ornate furnishings, and sacred objects'''. Likewise, without '''community spirit and pride''', they could not have '''maintained and passed down''' these traditions.
* The festival was '''originally held on the lunar calendar dates of July 18-20''', but in '''1870 (Meiji 3),''' it was '''changed to the current dates of August 18-20'''.
== Festival Floats (Yama) == The '''five districts of Tanabu''' (*Tanabu 5-chō*: '''Yokomukaichō, Ogawachō, Yanagimachi, Honmachi, and Shinmachi''') each have '''a traditional wooden festival float''', known as '''"Yama"''' (山車). These floats are '''black-lacquered, two-story structures'''.
- The '''lower level''' houses the '''musicians''' (*Noriko*, 囃子方). - The '''upper level''' carries '''the enshrined deity''' (*Goshintai*, 御神体). - Each '''Yama''' has '''unique carvings, lacquer paintings, roof styles, and decorative metal fittings'''. - The '''wheels are wooden''', and '''turning corners''' is difficult due to the '''height''' of the Yama, requiring '''skilled handlers (Teko, 梃子)''' to rotate the floats at intersections. * '''Thick ropes''' are attached to the '''Yama''', and they are '''pulled by hand'''.
The '''decorations change between day and night''': * '''Daytime:''' The '''Yama''' is adorned with '''embroidered curtains''' (*Ōmizuhiki-maku, Komizuhiki-maku, Mikaeri-maku, and Misu*). These textiles were traditionally '''ordered from Kyoto''' or '''imported from China via Nagasaki'''. * '''Nighttime:''' The curtains are replaced with '''illuminated painted panels (Gaku, 額)''', drawn by '''local artists''' and '''redesigned every year'''.
The festival '''groups (Kumi, 組)''' manage the '''Yama operation''', following a '''strict hierarchy''': * '''Kumi Leader (組頭, Kumitō)''' * '''Vice Leader (副組頭)''' * '''Sub-leaders (小頭, Kojirō)''' * '''Councilors (理事, Rijī)''' * '''Executive members (幹事, Kanjī)''' - Members '''"retire" at around age 40'''.
== List of Floats == === Lead Float: Inari Yama (稲荷山) === * '''Yokomukaichō Gōsen-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''[[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]] & a '''pair of white foxes''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Immortal riding a yellow crane'''
=== Second Float: Shōjō Yama (猩猩山) === * '''Ogawachō Giyū-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''[[:en:Shōjō|Shōjō]]''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Phoenix carrying an immortal'''
=== Third Float: Daikoku Yama (大黒山) === * '''Yanagimachi Kyōshin-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''[[:en:Daikokuten|Daikokuten]]''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Yoshimitsu receiving a secret tune'''
=== Fourth Float: Ebisu Yama (蛭子山) === * '''Honmachi & Tanabu-chō Myōsei-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Ebisu|ja|蛭子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebisu|qq=}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Great turtle carrying an immortal'''
=== Final Float: Kōro Hō (香爐峯) === * '''Shinmachi Shinsei-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''[[:en:Sei Shōnagon|Sei Shōnagon]]''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Oniwakamaru riding a koi fish'''
== Festival Music and Chants == The music features '''flutes, drums, gongs, and taiko'''. Two major musical styles: * '''Gion-bayashi''' (*Gion Music*): Played '''during daytime''', characterized by '''a melancholic, elegant melody'''. * '''Yamaya-re''' (*Nighttime music*): A '''fast-paced, elaborate flute melody''' played at night.
Different '''chants''' are used for float turns: * '''Gōsen-gumi (Inari Yama):''' "Todoro-tōnō" * '''Giyū-gumi (Shōjō Yama):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō" * '''Kyōshin-gumi (Daikoku Yama):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō" * '''Myōsei-gumi (Ebisu Yama):''' "Todoro-se-nō" * '''Shinsei-gumi (Kōro Hō):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō"
== Schedule == Held annually '''August 18-20'''.
== External Links == - [http://www.city.mutsu.lg.jp/ Tanabu Shrine Grand Festival – Mutsu City Official Website]
{{translated page|ja|田名部まつり|version=101873170|comment=Imported full ja history}}

revision.text:
[[File:Aomori- Tanabu Festival-m.jpg|thumb|270px|Tanabu Festival]] The '''Tanabu Festival''' (田名部まつり, *Tanabu Matsuri*) is the '''annual grand festival''' (*Reitaisai*) of '''Tanabu Shrine''' in '''[[:en:Mutsu, Aomori|Mutsu, Aomori]]'''. It has been designated as an '''intangible folk cultural property''' (*Mukei Minzoku Bunkazai*) of '''[[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]]'''.
== History and Background == * The '''origin''' of the '''Tanabu Festival''' is '''uncertain''', but it is said to have a '''history of over 370 years'''{{fact|date=August 2023}}.
* According to '''"Maki no Asatsuyu"''', written by the '''Edo-period traveler {{ill|Sugao Masumi|ja|菅江真澄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugao Masumi|qq=}}''' during his visit to Tanabu, the festival was already being held in a '''form similar to the present-day event by 1793''' (*Kansei 5*).
* '''Tanabu''' once housed the '''daikansho (magistrate's office)''' of the '''{{ill|Nanbu Domain|ja|南部藩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Domain|qq=}}''', making it the '''center of industry, economy, and culture''' in '''Shimokita'''. Although Tanabu itself did not have a '''seaport''', goods from '''[[:en:Kitamaebune|Kitamaebune]]''' ships docked in '''[[:en:Mutsu Bay|Mutsu Bay]]''' (*Ōminato Bay*), were transported '''via the Tanabu River''', and helped the town flourish.
* Shimokita exported '''Hiba cypress, abalone, sea cucumber, and kelp''' to the Kansai region, while rare goods arrived from various parts of Japan via the '''West-bound shipping route''' (*Saikairi Kairō*). Along with these goods, '''language and culture''' were also introduced to the region.
* Shimokita’s '''products were highly valued''' as '''luxury goods''' and sold as '''Nagasaki Tawaramono''' (*high-end exports to China*). This trade brought '''wealth''' to the townspeople. Though Shimokita was sometimes called a '''"barren land"''', the people could '''simply buy''' food when they couldn't grow their own. It is even said that the '''people of Shimokita did not struggle to eat''' and had the leisure to '''enjoy poetry, calligraphy, and education'''.
* Since the '''early Edo period''', many '''{{ill|Ōmi merchants|ja|近江Merchant|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi merchants|qq=}}''' were active in Shimokita, playing a major role in '''spreading language and culture'''. It is believed that through these merchants—who came from present-day '''Shiga Prefecture'''—the '''traditions of Kyoto's [[:en:Gion Matsuri|Gion Festival]]''' were introduced to Tanabu. This influence is evident in the '''festival floats (Yama)''' and '''festival music''', earning Tanabu Festival the nickname '''"Miyabi of the North"''' (*北のみやび*).
* The '''night festival music (Yamaya-re)''' resembles the '''Chichibu Night Festival''' and other '''Kantō-style''' festival melodies, suggesting influences from regions beyond Kyoto and Shiga.
* The '''daytime decorations''' reflect the '''extravagance of Kyoto’s Gion Festival''', with some items made '''in Kyoto''' or even '''imported from China and Persia'''. Meanwhile, the '''nighttime decorations''', such as the '''Mikaeri-e (rear-facing paintings)''', are influenced by '''Nebuta festivals''' from '''Tsugaru and Shimokita''' and are crafted locally.
* The origins of the '''tradition of Tanabu Festival''' remain unclear, but one thing is certain: without the '''economic power of the townspeople''', they could not have afforded '''lavish festival floats, ornate furnishings, and sacred objects'''. Likewise, without '''community spirit and pride''', they could not have '''maintained and passed down''' these traditions.
* The festival was '''originally held on the lunar calendar dates of July 18-20''', but in '''1870 (Meiji 3),''' it was '''changed to the current dates of August 18-20'''.
== Festival Floats (Yama) == The '''five districts of Tanabu''' (*Tanabu 5-chō*: '''Yokomukaichō, Ogawachō, Yanagimachi, Honmachi, and Shinmachi''') each have '''a traditional wooden festival float''', known as '''"Yama"''' (山車). These floats are '''black-lacquered, two-story structures'''.
- The '''lower level''' houses the '''musicians''' (*Noriko*, 囃子方). - The '''upper level''' carries '''the enshrined deity''' (*Goshintai*, 御神体). - Each '''Yama''' has '''unique carvings, lacquer paintings, roof styles, and decorative metal fittings'''. - The '''wheels are wooden''', and '''turning corners''' is difficult due to the '''height''' of the Yama, requiring '''skilled handlers (Teko, 梃子)''' to rotate the floats at intersections. * '''Thick ropes''' are attached to the '''Yama''', and they are '''pulled by hand'''.
The '''decorations change between day and night''': * '''Daytime:''' The '''Yama''' is adorned with '''embroidered curtains''' (*Ōmizuhiki-maku, Komizuhiki-maku, Mikaeri-maku, and Misu*). These textiles were traditionally '''ordered from Kyoto''' or '''imported from China via Nagasaki'''. * '''Nighttime:''' The curtains are replaced with '''illuminated painted panels (Gaku, 額)''', drawn by '''local artists''' and '''redesigned every year'''.
The festival '''groups (Kumi, 組)''' manage the '''Yama operation''', following a '''strict hierarchy''': * '''Kumi Leader (組頭, Kumitō)''' * '''Vice Leader (副組頭)''' * '''Sub-leaders (小頭, Kojirō)''' * '''Councilors (理事, Rijī)''' * '''Executive members (幹事, Kanjī)''' - Members '''"retire" at around age 40'''.
== List of Floats == === Lead Float: Inari Yama (稲荷山) === * '''Yokomukaichō Gōsen-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''[[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]] & a '''pair of white foxes''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Immortal riding a yellow crane'''
=== Second Float: Shōjō Yama (猩猩山) === * '''Ogawachō Giyū-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''[[:en:Shōjō|Shōjō]]''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Phoenix carrying an immortal'''
=== Third Float: Daikoku Yama (大黒山) === * '''Yanagimachi Kyōshin-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''[[:en:Daikokuten|Daikokuten]]''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Yoshimitsu receiving a secret tune'''
=== Fourth Float: Ebisu Yama (蛭子山) === * '''Honmachi & Tanabu-chō Myōsei-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Ebisu|ja|蛭子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebisu|qq=}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Great turtle carrying an immortal'''
=== Final Float: Kōro Hō (香爐峯) === * '''Shinmachi Shinsei-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''[[:en:Sei Shōnagon|Sei Shōnagon]]''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Oniwakamaru riding a koi fish'''
== Festival Music and Chants == The music features '''flutes, drums, gongs, and taiko'''. Two major musical styles: * '''Gion-bayashi''' (*Gion Music*): Played '''during daytime''', characterized by '''a melancholic, elegant melody'''. * '''Yamaya-re''' (*Nighttime music*): A '''fast-paced, elaborate flute melody''' played at night.
Different '''chants''' are used for float turns: * '''Gōsen-gumi (Inari Yama):''' "Todoro-tōnō" * '''Giyū-gumi (Shōjō Yama):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō" * '''Kyōshin-gumi (Daikoku Yama):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō" * '''Myōsei-gumi (Ebisu Yama):''' "Todoro-se-nō" * '''Shinsei-gumi (Kōro Hō):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō"
== Schedule == Held annually '''August 18-20'''.
== External Links == - [http://www.city.mutsu.lg.jp/ Tanabu Shrine Grand Festival – Mutsu City Official Website]

Likewise, without '''community spirit and pride''', they could not have '''maintained and passed down''' these traditions.
* The festival was '''originally held on the lunar calendar dates of July 18-20''', but in '''1870 (Meiji 3),''' it was '''changed to the current dates of August 18-20'''.
== Festival Floats (Yama) == The '''five districts of Tanabu''' (*Tanabu 5-chō*: '''Yokomukaichō, Ogawachō, Yanagimachi, Honmachi, and Shinmachi''') each have '''a traditional wooden festival float''', known as '''"Yama"''' (山車). These floats are '''black-lacquered, two-story structures'''.
- The '''lower level''' houses the '''musicians''' (*Noriko*, 囃子方). - The '''upper level''' carries '''the enshrined deity''' (*Goshintai*, 御神体). - Each '''Yama''' has '''unique carvings, lacquer paintings, roof styles, and decorative metal fittings'''. - The '''wheels are wooden''', and '''turning corners''' is difficult due to the '''height''' of the Yama, requiring '''skilled handlers (Teko, 梃子)''' to rotate the floats at intersections. * '''Thick ropes''' are attached to the '''Yama''', and they are '''pulled by hand'''.
The '''decorations change between day and night''': * '''Daytime:''' The '''Yama''' is adorned with '''embroidered curtains''' (*Ōmizuhiki-maku, Komizuhiki-maku, Mikaeri-maku, and Misu*). These textiles were traditionally '''ordered from Kyoto''' or '''imported from China via Nagasaki'''. * '''Nighttime:''' The curtains are replaced with '''illuminated painted panels (Gaku, 額)''', drawn by '''local artists''' and '''redesigned every year'''.
The festival '''groups (Kumi, 組)''' manage the '''Yama operation''', following a '''strict hierarchy''': * '''Kumi Leader (組頭, Kumitō)''' * '''Vice Leader (副組頭)''' * '''Sub-leaders (小頭, Kojirō)''' * '''Councilors (理事, Rijī)''' * '''Executive members (幹事, Kanjī)''' - Members '''"retire" at around age 40'''.
== List of Floats == === Lead Float: Inari Yama (稲荷山) === * '''Yokomukaichō Gōsen-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''[[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]] & a '''pair of white foxes''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Immortal riding a yellow crane'''
=== Second Float: Shōjō Yama (猩猩山) === * '''Ogawachō Giyū-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''[[:en:Shōjō|Shōjō]]''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Phoenix carrying an immortal'''
=== Third Float: Daikoku Yama (大黒山) === * '''Yanagimachi Kyōshin-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''[[:en:Daikokuten|Daikokuten]]''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Yoshimitsu receiving a secret tune'''
=== Fourth Float: Ebisu Yama (蛭子山) === * '''Honmachi & Tanabu-chō Myōsei-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Ebisu|ja|蛭子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebisu|qq=}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Great turtle carrying an immortal'''
=== Final Float: Kōro Hō (香爐峯) === * '''Shinmachi Shinsei-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''[[:en:Sei Shōnagon|Sei Shōnagon]]''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Oniwakamaru riding a koi fish'''
== Festival Music and Chants == The music features '''flutes, drums, gongs, and taiko'''. Two major musical styles: * '''Gion-bayashi''' (*Gion Music*): Played '''during daytime''', characterized by '''a melancholic, elegant melody'''. * '''Yamaya-re''' (*Nighttime music*): A '''fast-paced, elaborate flute melody''' played at night.
Different '''chants''' are used for float turns: * '''Gōsen-gumi (Inari Yama):''' "Todoro-tōnō" * '''Giyū-gumi (Shōjō Yama):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō" * '''Kyōshin-gumi (Daikoku Yama):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō" * '''Myōsei-gumi (Ebisu Yama):''' "Todoro-se-nō" * '''Shinsei-gumi (Kōro Hō):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō"
== Schedule == Held annually '''August 18-20'''.
== External Links == - [http://www.city.mutsu.lg.jp/ Tanabu Shrine Grand Festival – Mutsu City Official Website]

revision.text:
[[File:Aomori- Tanabu Festival-m.jpg|thumb|270px|Tanabu Festival]] The '''Tanabu Festival''' (田名部まつり, *Tanabu Matsuri*) is the '''annual grand festival''' (*Reitaisai*) of '''Tanabu Shrine''' in '''[[:en:Mutsu, Aomori|Mutsu, Aomori]]'''. It has been designated as an '''intangible folk cultural property''' (*Mukei Minzoku Bunkazai*) of '''[[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]]'''.
== History and Background == * The '''origin''' of the '''Tanabu Festival''' is '''uncertain''', but it is said to have a '''history of over 370 years'''{{fact|date=August 2023}}.
* According to '''"Maki no Asatsuyu"''', written by the '''Edo-period traveler {{ill|Sugao Masumi|ja|菅江真澄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugao Masumi|qq=}}''' during his visit to Tanabu, the festival was already being held in a '''form similar to the present-day event by 1793''' (*Kansei 5*).
* '''Tanabu''' once housed the '''daikansho (magistrate's office)''' of the '''{{ill|Nanbu Domain|ja|南部藩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Domain|qq=}}''', making it the '''center of industry, economy, and culture''' in '''Shimokita'''. Although Tanabu itself did not have a '''seaport''', goods from '''[[:en:Kitamaebune|Kitamaebune]]''' ships docked in '''[[:en:Mutsu Bay|Mutsu Bay]]''' (*Ōminato Bay*), were transported '''via the Tanabu River''', and helped the town flourish.
* Shimokita exported '''Hiba cypress, abalone, sea cucumber, and kelp''' to the Kansai region, while rare goods arrived from various parts of Japan via the '''West-bound shipping route''' (*Saikairi Kairō*). Along with these goods, '''language and culture''' were also introduced to the region.
* Shimokita’s '''products were highly valued''' as '''luxury goods''' and sold as '''Nagasaki Tawaramono''' (*high-end exports to China*). This trade brought '''wealth''' to the townspeople. Though Shimokita was sometimes called a '''"barren land"''', the people could '''simply buy''' food when they couldn't grow their own. It is even said that the '''people of Shimokita did not struggle to eat''' and had the leisure to '''enjoy poetry, calligraphy, and education'''.
* Since the '''early Edo period''', many '''{{ill|Ōmi merchants|ja|近江Merchant|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi merchants|qq=}}''' were active in Shimokita, playing a major role in '''spreading language and culture'''. It is believed that through these merchants—who came from present-day '''Shiga Prefecture'''—the '''traditions of Kyoto's [[:en:Gion Matsuri|Gion Festival]]''' were introduced to Tanabu. This influence is evident in the '''festival floats (Yama)''' and '''festival music''', earning Tanabu Festival the nickname '''"Miyabi of the North"''' (*北のみやび*).
* The '''night festival music (Yamaya-re)''' resembles the '''Chichibu Night Festival''' and other '''Kantō-style''' festival melodies, suggesting influences from regions beyond Kyoto and Shiga.
* The '''daytime decorations''' reflect the '''extravagance of Kyoto’s Gion Festival''', with some items made '''in Kyoto''' or even '''imported from China and Persia'''. Meanwhile, the '''nighttime decorations''', such as the '''Mikaeri-e (rear-facing paintings)''', are influenced by '''Nebuta festivals''' from '''Tsugaru and Shimokita''' and are crafted locally.
* The origins of the '''tradition of Tanabu Festival''' remain unclear, but one thing is certain: without the '''economic power of the townspeople''', they could not have afforded '''lavish festival floats, ornate furnishings, and sacred objects'''. Likewise, without '''community spirit and pride''', they could not have '''maintained and passed down''' these traditions.
* The festival was '''originally held on the lunar calendar dates of July 18-20''', but in '''1870 (Meiji 3),''' it was '''changed to the current dates of August 18-20'''.
== Festival Floats (Yama) == The '''five districts of Tanabu''' (*Tanabu 5-chō*: '''Yokomukaichō, Ogawachō, Yanagimachi, Honmachi, and Shinmachi''') each have '''a traditional wooden festival float''', known as '''"Yama"''' (山車). These floats are '''black-lacquered, two-story structures'''.
- The '''lower level''' houses the '''musicians''' (*Noriko*, 囃子方). - The '''upper level''' carries '''the enshrined deity''' (*Goshintai*, 御神体). - Each '''Yama''' has '''unique carvings, lacquer paintings, roof styles, and decorative metal fittings'''. - The '''wheels are wooden''', and '''turning corners''' is difficult due to the '''height''' of the Yama, requiring '''skilled handlers (Teko, 梃子)''' to rotate the floats at intersections. * '''Thick ropes''' are attached to the '''Yama''', and they are '''pulled by hand'''.
The '''decorations change between day and night''': * '''Daytime:''' The '''Yama''' is adorned with '''embroidered curtains''' (*Ōmizuhiki-maku, Komizuhiki-maku, Mikaeri-maku, and Misu*). These textiles were traditionally '''ordered from Kyoto''' or '''imported from China via Nagasaki'''. * '''Nighttime:''' The curtains are replaced with '''illuminated painted panels (Gaku, 額)''', drawn by '''local artists''' and '''redesigned every year'''.
The festival '''groups (Kumi, 組)''' manage the '''Yama operation''', following a '''strict hierarchy''': * '''Kumi Leader (組頭, Kumitō)''' * '''Vice Leader (副組頭)''' * '''Sub-leaders (小頭, Kojirō)''' * '''Councilors (理事, Rijī)''' * '''Executive members (幹事, Kanjī)''' - Members '''"retire" at around age 40'''.
== List of Floats == === Lead Float: Inari Yama (稲荷山) === * '''Yokomukaichō Gōsen-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''[[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]] & a '''pair of white foxes''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Immortal riding a yellow crane'''
=== Second Float: Shōjō Yama (猩猩山) === * '''Ogawachō Giyū-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''[[:en:Shōjō|Shōjō]]''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Phoenix carrying an immortal'''
=== Third Float: Daikoku Yama (大黒山) === * '''Yanagimachi Kyōshin-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''[[:en:Daikokuten|Daikokuten]]''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Yoshimitsu receiving a secret tune'''
=== Fourth Float: Ebisu Yama (蛭子山) === * '''Honmachi & Tanabu-chō Myōsei-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Ebisu|ja|蛭子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebisu|qq=}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Great turtle carrying an immortal'''
=== Final Float: Kōro Hō (香爐峯) === * '''Shinmachi Shinsei-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''[[:en:Sei Shōnagon|Sei Shōnagon]]''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Oniwakamaru riding a koi fish'''
== Festival Music and Chants == The music features '''flutes, drums, gongs, and taiko'''. Two major musical styles: * '''Gion-bayashi''' (*Gion Music*): Played '''during daytime''', characterized by '''a melancholic, elegant melody'''. * '''Yamaya-re''' (*Nighttime music*): A '''fast-paced, elaborate flute melody''' played at night.
Different '''chants''' are used for float turns: * '''Gōsen-gumi (Inari Yama):''' "Todoro-tōnō" * '''Giyū-gumi (Shōjō Yama):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō" * '''Kyōshin-gumi (Daikoku Yama):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō" * '''Myōsei-gumi (Ebisu Yama):''' "Todoro-se-nō" * '''Shinsei-gumi (Kōro Hō):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō"
== Schedule == Held annually '''August 18-20'''.
== External Links == - [http://www.city.mutsu.lg.jp/ Tanabu Shrine Grand Festival – Mutsu City Official Website]

The '''decorations change between day and night''': * '''Daytime:''' The '''Yama''' is adorned with '''embroidered curtains''' (*Ōmizuhiki-maku, Komizuhiki-maku, Mikaeri-maku, and Misu*). These textiles were traditionally '''ordered from Kyoto''' or '''imported from China via Nagasaki'''. * '''Nighttime:''' The curtains are replaced with '''illuminated painted panels (Gaku, 額)''', drawn by '''local artists''' and '''redesigned every year'''.
The festival '''groups (Kumi, 組)''' manage the '''Yama operation''', following a '''strict hierarchy''': * '''Kumi Leader (組頭, Kumitō)''' * '''Vice Leader (副組頭)''' * '''Sub-leaders (小頭, Kojirō)''' * '''Councilors (理事, Rijī)''' * '''Executive members (幹事, Kanjī)''' - Members '''"retire" at around age 40'''.
== List of Floats == === Lead Float: Inari Yama (稲荷山) === * '''Yokomukaichō Gōsen-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''[[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]] & a '''pair of white foxes''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Immortal riding a yellow crane'''
=== Second Float: Shōjō Yama (猩猩山) === * '''Ogawachō Giyū-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''[[:en:Shōjō|Shōjō]]''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Phoenix carrying an immortal'''
=== Third Float: Daikoku Yama (大黒山) === * '''Yanagimachi Kyōshin-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''[[:en:Daikokuten|Daikokuten]]''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Yoshimitsu receiving a secret tune'''
=== Fourth Float: Ebisu Yama (蛭子山) === * '''Honmachi & Tanabu-chō Myōsei-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Ebisu|ja|蛭子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebisu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヒルコ}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Great turtle carrying an immortal'''
=== Final Float: Kōro Hō (香爐峯) === * '''Shinmachi Shinsei-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''[[:en:Sei Shōnagon|Sei Shōnagon]]''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Oniwakamaru riding a koi fish'''
== Festival Music and Chants == The music features '''flutes, drums, gongs, and taiko'''. Two major musical styles: * '''Gion-bayashi''' (*Gion Music*): Played '''during daytime''', characterized by '''a melancholic, elegant melody'''. * '''Yamaya-re''' (*Nighttime music*): A '''fast-paced, elaborate flute melody''' played at night.
Different '''chants''' are used for float turns: * '''Gōsen-gumi (Inari Yama):''' "Todoro-tōnō" * '''Giyū-gumi (Shōjō Yama):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō" * '''Kyōshin-gumi (Daikoku Yama):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō" * '''Myōsei-gumi (Ebisu Yama):''' "Todoro-se-nō" * '''Shinsei-gumi (Kōro Hō):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō"
== Schedule == Held annually '''August 18-20'''.
== External Links == - [http://www.city.mutsu.lg.jp/ Tanabu Shrine Grand Festival – Mutsu City Official Website]

revision.comment: Merge [[Category:Festivals of Aomori Prefecture]] into [[Category:Festivals in Aomori Prefecture]] via bot
revision.text:
[[File:Aomori- Tanabu Festival-m.jpg|thumb|270px|Tanabu Festival]] The '''Tanabu Festival''' (田名部まつり, *Tanabu Matsuri*) is the '''annual grand festival''' (*Reitaisai*) of '''Tanabu Shrine''' in '''[[:en:Mutsu, Aomori|Mutsu, Aomori]]'''. It has been designated as an '''intangible folk cultural property''' (*Mukei Minzoku Bunkazai*) of '''[[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]]'''.
== History and Background == * The '''origin''' of the '''Tanabu Festival''' is '''uncertain''', but it is said to have a '''history of over 370 years'''{{fact|date=August 2023}}.
* According to '''"Maki no Asatsuyu"''', written by the '''Edo-period traveler {{ill|Sugao Masumi|ja|菅江真澄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugao Masumi|qq=}}''' during his visit to Tanabu, the festival was already being held in a '''form similar to the present-day event by 1793''' (*Kansei 5*).
* '''Tanabu''' once housed the '''daikansho (magistrate's office)''' of the '''{{ill|Nanbu Domain|ja|南部藩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Domain|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 盛岡藩}}''', making it the '''center of industry, economy, and culture''' in '''Shimokita'''. Although Tanabu itself did not have a '''seaport''', goods from '''[[:en:Kitamaebune|Kitamaebune]]''' ships docked in '''[[:en:Mutsu Bay|Mutsu Bay]]''' (*Ōminato Bay*), were transported '''via the Tanabu River''', and helped the town flourish.
* Shimokita exported '''Hiba cypress, abalone, sea cucumber, and kelp''' to the Kansai region, while rare goods arrived from various parts of Japan via the '''West-bound shipping route''' (*Saikairi Kairō*). Along with these goods, '''language and culture''' were also introduced to the region.
* Shimokita’s '''products were highly valued''' as '''luxury goods''' and sold as '''Nagasaki Tawaramono''' (*high-end exports to China*). This trade brought '''wealth''' to the townspeople. Though Shimokita was sometimes called a '''"barren land"''', the people could '''simply buy''' food when they couldn't grow their own. It is even said that the '''people of Shimokita did not struggle to eat''' and had the leisure to '''enjoy poetry, calligraphy, and education'''.
* Since the '''early Edo period''', many '''{{ill|Ōmi merchants|ja|近江Merchant|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi merchants|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''' were active in Shimokita, playing a major role in '''spreading language and culture'''. It is believed that through these merchants—who came from present-day '''Shiga Prefecture'''—the '''traditions of Kyoto's [[:en:Gion Matsuri|Gion Festival]]''' were introduced to Tanabu. This influence is evident in the '''festival floats (Yama)''' and '''festival music''', earning Tanabu Festival the nickname '''"Miyabi of the North"''' (*北のみやび*).
* The '''night festival music (Yamaya-re)''' resembles the '''Chichibu Night Festival''' and other '''Kantō-style''' festival melodies, suggesting influences from regions beyond Kyoto and Shiga.
* The '''daytime decorations''' reflect the '''extravagance of Kyoto’s Gion Festival''', with some items made '''in Kyoto''' or even '''imported from China and Persia'''. Meanwhile, the '''nighttime decorations''', such as the '''Mikaeri-e (rear-facing paintings)''', are influenced by '''Nebuta festivals''' from '''Tsugaru and Shimokita''' and are crafted locally.
* The origins of the '''tradition of Tanabu Festival''' remain unclear, but one thing is certain: without the '''economic power of the townspeople''', they could not have afforded '''lavish festival floats, ornate furnishings, and sacred objects'''. Likewise, without '''community spirit and pride''', they could not have '''maintained and passed down''' these traditions.
* The festival was '''originally held on the lunar calendar dates of July 18-20''', but in '''1870 (Meiji 3),''' it was '''changed to the current dates of August 18-20'''.
== Festival Floats (Yama) == The '''five districts of Tanabu''' (*Tanabu 5-chō*: '''Yokomukaichō, Ogawachō, Yanagimachi, Honmachi, and Shinmachi''') each have '''a traditional wooden festival float''', known as '''"Yama"''' (山車). These floats are '''black-lacquered, two-story structures'''.
- The '''lower level''' houses the '''musicians''' (*Noriko*, 囃子方). - The '''upper level''' carries '''the enshrined deity''' (*Goshintai*, 御神体). - Each '''Yama''' has '''unique carvings, lacquer paintings, roof styles, and decorative metal fittings'''. - The '''wheels are wooden''', and '''turning corners''' is difficult due to the '''height''' of the Yama, requiring '''skilled handlers (Teko, 梃子)''' to rotate the floats at intersections. * '''Thick ropes''' are attached to the '''Yama''', and they are '''pulled by hand'''.
The '''decorations change between day and night''': * '''Daytime:''' The '''Yama''' is adorned with '''embroidered curtains''' (*Ōmizuhiki-maku, Komizuhiki-maku, Mikaeri-maku, and Misu*). These textiles were traditionally '''ordered from Kyoto''' or '''imported from China via Nagasaki'''. * '''Nighttime:''' The curtains are replaced with '''illuminated painted panels (Gaku, 額)''', drawn by '''local artists''' and '''redesigned every year'''.
The festival '''groups (Kumi, 組)''' manage the '''Yama operation''', following a '''strict hierarchy''': * '''Kumi Leader (組頭, Kumitō)''' * '''Vice Leader (副組頭)''' * '''Sub-leaders (小頭, Kojirō)''' * '''Councilors (理事, Rijī)''' * '''Executive members (幹事, Kanjī)''' - Members '''"retire" at around age 40'''.
== List of Floats == === Lead Float: Inari Yama (稲荷山) === * '''Yokomukaichō Gōsen-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''[[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]] & a '''pair of white foxes''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Immortal riding a yellow crane'''
=== Second Float: Shōjō Yama (猩猩山) === * '''Ogawachō Giyū-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''[[:en:Shōjō|Shōjō]]''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Phoenix carrying an immortal'''
=== Third Float: Daikoku Yama (大黒山) === * '''Yanagimachi Kyōshin-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''[[:en:Daikokuten|Daikokuten]]''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Yoshimitsu receiving a secret tune'''
=== Fourth Float: Ebisu Yama (蛭子山) === * '''Honmachi & Tanabu-chō Myōsei-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Ebisu|ja|蛭子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebisu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヒルコ}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Great turtle carrying an immortal'''
=== Final Float: Kōro Hō (香爐峯) === * '''Shinmachi Shinsei-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''[[:en:Sei Shōnagon|Sei Shōnagon]]''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Oniwakamaru riding a koi fish'''
== Festival Music and Chants == The music features '''flutes, drums, gongs, and taiko'''. Two major musical styles: * '''Gion-bayashi''' (*Gion Music*): Played '''during daytime''', characterized by '''a melancholic, elegant melody'''. * '''Yamaya-re''' (*Nighttime music*): A '''fast-paced, elaborate flute melody''' played at night.
Different '''chants''' are used for float turns: * '''Gōsen-gumi (Inari Yama):''' "Todoro-tōnō" * '''Giyū-gumi (Shōjō Yama):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō" * '''Kyōshin-gumi (Daikoku Yama):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō" * '''Myōsei-gumi (Ebisu Yama):''' "Todoro-se-nō" * '''Shinsei-gumi (Kōro Hō):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō"
== Schedule == Held annually '''August 18-20'''.
== External Links == - [http://www.city.mutsu.lg.jp/ Tanabu Shrine Grand Festival – Mutsu City Official Website]

These textiles were traditionally '''ordered from Kyoto''' or '''imported from China via Nagasaki'''. * '''Nighttime:''' The curtains are replaced with '''illuminated painted panels (Gaku, 額)''', drawn by '''local artists''' and '''redesigned every year'''.
The festival '''groups (Kumi, 組)''' manage the '''Yama operation''', following a '''strict hierarchy''': * '''Kumi Leader (組頭, Kumitō)''' * '''Vice Leader (副組頭)''' * '''Sub-leaders (小頭, Kojirō)''' * '''Councilors (理事, Rijī)''' * '''Executive members (幹事, Kanjī)''' - Members '''"retire" at around age 40'''.
== List of Floats == === Lead Float: Inari Yama (稲荷山) === * '''Yokomukaichō Gōsen-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''[[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]] & a '''pair of white foxes''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Immortal riding a yellow crane'''
=== Second Float: Shōjō Yama (猩猩山) === * '''Ogawachō Giyū-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''[[:en:Shōjō|Shōjō]]''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Phoenix carrying an immortal'''
=== Third Float: Daikoku Yama (大黒山) === * '''Yanagimachi Kyōshin-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''[[:en:Daikokuten|Daikokuten]]''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Yoshimitsu receiving a secret tune'''
=== Fourth Float: Ebisu Yama (蛭子山) === * '''Honmachi & Tanabu-chō Myōsei-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Ebisu|ja|蛭子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebisu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヒルコ}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Great turtle carrying an immortal'''
=== Final Float: Kōro Hō (香爐峯) === * '''Shinmachi Shinsei-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''[[:en:Sei Shōnagon|Sei Shōnagon]]''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Oniwakamaru riding a koi fish'''
== Festival Music and Chants == The music features '''flutes, drums, gongs, and taiko'''. Two major musical styles: * '''Gion-bayashi''' (*Gion Music*): Played '''during daytime''', characterized by '''a melancholic, elegant melody'''. * '''Yamaya-re''' (*Nighttime music*): A '''fast-paced, elaborate flute melody''' played at night.
Different '''chants''' are used for float turns: * '''Gōsen-gumi (Inari Yama):''' "Todoro-tōnō" * '''Giyū-gumi (Shōjō Yama):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō" * '''Kyōshin-gumi (Daikoku Yama):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō" * '''Myōsei-gumi (Ebisu Yama):''' "Todoro-se-nō" * '''Shinsei-gumi (Kōro Hō):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō"
== Schedule == Held annually '''August 18-20'''.
== External Links == - [http://www.city.mutsu.lg.jp/ Tanabu Shrine Grand Festival – Mutsu City Official Website]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "paes with no wikidata" to "Pages with no Wikidata"
revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:50, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Aomori- Tanabu Festival-m.jpg|thumb|270px|Tanabu Festival]] The '''Tanabu Festival''' (田名部まつり, *Tanabu Matsuri*) is the '''annual grand festival''' (*Reitaisai*) of '''Tanabu Shrine''' in '''[[:en:Mutsu, Aomori|Mutsu, Aomori]]'''. It has been designated as an '''intangible folk cultural property''' (*Mukei Minzoku Bunkazai*) of '''[[:en:Aomori Prefecture|Aomori Prefecture]]'''.
== History and Background == * The '''origin''' of the '''Tanabu Festival''' is '''uncertain''', but it is said to have a '''history of over 370 years'''{{fact|date=August 2023}}.
* According to '''"Maki no Asatsuyu"''', written by the '''Edo-period traveler {{ill|Sugao Masumi|ja|菅江真澄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugao Masumi|qq=}}''' during his visit to Tanabu, the festival was already being held in a '''form similar to the present-day event by 1793''' (*Kansei 5*).
* '''Tanabu''' once housed the '''daikansho (magistrate's office)''' of the '''{{ill|Nanbu Domain|ja|南部藩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nanbu Domain|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 盛岡藩}}''', making it the '''center of industry, economy, and culture''' in '''Shimokita'''. Although Tanabu itself did not have a '''seaport''', goods from '''[[:en:Kitamaebune|Kitamaebune]]''' ships docked in '''[[:en:Mutsu Bay|Mutsu Bay]]''' (*Ōminato Bay*), were transported '''via the Tanabu River''', and helped the town flourish.
* Shimokita exported '''Hiba cypress, abalone, sea cucumber, and kelp''' to the Kansai region, while rare goods arrived from various parts of Japan via the '''West-bound shipping route''' (*Saikairi Kairō*). Along with these goods, '''language and culture''' were also introduced to the region.
* Shimokita’s '''products were highly valued''' as '''luxury goods''' and sold as '''Nagasaki Tawaramono''' (*high-end exports to China*). This trade brought '''wealth''' to the townspeople. Though Shimokita was sometimes called a '''"barren land"''', the people could '''simply buy''' food when they couldn't grow their own. It is even said that the '''people of Shimokita did not struggle to eat''' and had the leisure to '''enjoy poetry, calligraphy, and education'''.
* Since the '''early Edo period''', many '''{{ill|Ōmi merchants|ja|近江Merchant|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi merchants|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''' were active in Shimokita, playing a major role in '''spreading language and culture'''. It is believed that through these merchants—who came from present-day '''Shiga Prefecture'''—the '''traditions of Kyoto's [[:en:Gion Matsuri|Gion Festival]]''' were introduced to Tanabu. This influence is evident in the '''festival floats (Yama)''' and '''festival music''', earning Tanabu Festival the nickname '''"Miyabi of the North"''' (*北のみやび*).
* The '''night festival music (Yamaya-re)''' resembles the '''Chichibu Night Festival''' and other '''Kantō-style''' festival melodies, suggesting influences from regions beyond Kyoto and Shiga.
* The '''daytime decorations''' reflect the '''extravagance of Kyoto’s Gion Festival''', with some items made '''in Kyoto''' or even '''imported from China and Persia'''. Meanwhile, the '''nighttime decorations''', such as the '''Mikaeri-e (rear-facing paintings)''', are influenced by '''Nebuta festivals''' from '''Tsugaru and Shimokita''' and are crafted locally.
* The origins of the '''tradition of Tanabu Festival''' remain unclear, but one thing is certain: without the '''economic power of the townspeople''', they could not have afforded '''lavish festival floats, ornate furnishings, and sacred objects'''. Likewise, without '''community spirit and pride''', they could not have '''maintained and passed down''' these traditions.
* The festival was '''originally held on the lunar calendar dates of July 18-20''', but in '''1870 (Meiji 3),''' it was '''changed to the current dates of August 18-20'''.
== Festival Floats (Yama) == The '''five districts of Tanabu''' (*Tanabu 5-chō*: '''Yokomukaichō, Ogawachō, Yanagimachi, Honmachi, and Shinmachi''') each have '''a traditional wooden festival float''', known as '''"Yama"''' (山車). These floats are '''black-lacquered, two-story structures'''.
- The '''lower level''' houses the '''musicians''' (*Noriko*, 囃子方). - The '''upper level''' carries '''the enshrined deity''' (*Goshintai*, 御神体). - Each '''Yama''' has '''unique carvings, lacquer paintings, roof styles, and decorative metal fittings'''. - The '''wheels are wooden''', and '''turning corners''' is difficult due to the '''height''' of the Yama, requiring '''skilled handlers (Teko, 梃子)''' to rotate the floats at intersections. * '''Thick ropes''' are attached to the '''Yama''', and they are '''pulled by hand'''.
The '''decorations change between day and night''': * '''Daytime:''' The '''Yama''' is adorned with '''embroidered curtains''' (*Ōmizuhiki-maku, Komizuhiki-maku, Mikaeri-maku, and Misu*). These textiles were traditionally '''ordered from Kyoto''' or '''imported from China via Nagasaki'''. * '''Nighttime:''' The curtains are replaced with '''illuminated painted panels (Gaku, 額)''', drawn by '''local artists''' and '''redesigned every year'''.
The festival '''groups (Kumi, 組)''' manage the '''Yama operation''', following a '''strict hierarchy''': * '''Kumi Leader (組頭, Kumitō)''' * '''Vice Leader (副組頭)''' * '''Sub-leaders (小頭, Kojirō)''' * '''Councilors (理事, Rijī)''' * '''Executive members (幹事, Kanjī)''' - Members '''"retire" at around age 40'''.
== List of Floats == === Lead Float: Inari Yama (稲荷山) === * '''Yokomukaichō Gōsen-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''[[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]] & a '''pair of white foxes''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Immortal riding a yellow crane'''
=== Second Float: Shōjō Yama (猩猩山) === * '''Ogawachō Giyū-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''[[:en:Shōjō|Shōjō]]''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Phoenix carrying an immortal'''
=== Third Float: Daikoku Yama (大黒山) === * '''Yanagimachi Kyōshin-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''[[:en:Daikokuten|Daikokuten]]''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Yoshimitsu receiving a secret tune'''
=== Fourth Float: Ebisu Yama (蛭子山) === * '''Honmachi & Tanabu-chō Myōsei-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Ebisu|ja|蛭子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebisu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヒルコ}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Great turtle carrying an immortal'''
=== Final Float: Kōro Hō (香爐峯) === * '''Shinmachi Shinsei-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''[[:en:Sei Shōnagon|Sei Shōnagon]]''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Oniwakamaru riding a koi fish'''
== Festival Music and Chants == The music features '''flutes, drums, gongs, and taiko'''. Two major musical styles: * '''Gion-bayashi''' (*Gion Music*): Played '''during daytime''', characterized by '''a melancholic, elegant melody'''. * '''Yamaya-re''' (*Nighttime music*): A '''fast-paced, elaborate flute melody''' played at night.
Different '''chants''' are used for float turns: * '''Gōsen-gumi (Inari Yama):''' "Todoro-tōnō" * '''Giyū-gumi (Shōjō Yama):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō" * '''Kyōshin-gumi (Daikoku Yama):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō" * '''Myōsei-gumi (Ebisu Yama):''' "Todoro-se-nō" * '''Shinsei-gumi (Kōro Hō):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō"
== Schedule == Held annually '''August 18-20'''.
== External Links == - [http://www.city.mutsu.lg.jp/ Tanabu Shrine Grand Festival – Mutsu City Official Website]

These textiles were traditionally '''ordered from Kyoto''' or '''imported from China via Nagasaki'''. * '''Nighttime:''' The curtains are replaced with '''illuminated painted panels (Gaku, 額)''', drawn by '''local artists''' and '''redesigned every year'''.
The festival '''groups (Kumi, 組)''' manage the '''Yama operation''', following a '''strict hierarchy''': * '''Kumi Leader (組頭, Kumitō)''' * '''Vice Leader (副組頭)''' * '''Sub-leaders (小頭, Kojirō)''' * '''Councilors (理事, Rijī)''' * '''Executive members (幹事, Kanjī)''' - Members '''"retire" at around age 40'''.
== List of Floats == === Lead Float: Inari Yama (稲荷山) === * '''Yokomukaichō Gōsen-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''[[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]] & a '''pair of white foxes''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Immortal riding a yellow crane'''
=== Second Float: Shōjō Yama (猩猩山) === * '''Ogawachō Giyū-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''[[:en:Shōjō|Shōjō]]''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Phoenix carrying an immortal'''
=== Third Float: Daikoku Yama (大黒山) === * '''Yanagimachi Kyōshin-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''[[:en:Daikokuten|Daikokuten]]''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Yoshimitsu receiving a secret tune'''
=== Fourth Float: Ebisu Yama (蛭子山) === * '''Honmachi & Tanabu-chō Myōsei-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''{{ill|Ebisu|ja|蛭子|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebisu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヒルコ}}''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Great turtle carrying an immortal'''
=== Final Float: Kōro Hō (香爐峯) === * '''Shinmachi Shinsei-gumi''' * '''Deity:''' '''[[:en:Sei Shōnagon|Sei Shōnagon]]''' * '''Rear Panel:''' '''Oniwakamaru riding a koi fish'''
== Festival Music and Chants == The music features '''flutes, drums, gongs, and taiko'''. Two major musical styles: * '''Gion-bayashi''' (*Gion Music*): Played '''during daytime''', characterized by '''a melancholic, elegant melody'''. * '''Yamaya-re''' (*Nighttime music*): A '''fast-paced, elaborate flute melody''' played at night.
Different '''chants''' are used for float turns: * '''Gōsen-gumi (Inari Yama):''' "Todoro-tōnō" * '''Giyū-gumi (Shōjō Yama):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō" * '''Kyōshin-gumi (Daikoku Yama):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō" * '''Myōsei-gumi (Ebisu Yama):''' "Todoro-se-nō" * '''Shinsei-gumi (Kōro Hō):''' "Yadeko-rāse-nō"
== Schedule == Held annually '''August 18-20'''.
== External Links == - [http://www.city.mutsu.lg.jp/ Tanabu Shrine Grand Festival – Mutsu City Official Website]
